LEN 52912
Disk survey (threshold 85%): 228 GB: 61%
Heat: rbk-15-mvp-release-qualification (₣BB)
Status: racing
Created: 260426


## Paces

### rbw-tP-first-run (₢BBAAX) [rough]

## Character

First end-to-end execution of the gauntlet suite via rbw-tP against actual fresh state. The bug class this heat exists to catch — silent first-build assumptions — surfaces here in its remaining form: case-level probes have caught what they can during walkthrough and fixture construction; this pace exposes the cross-section bugs only the full assembled sequence reveals.

**Wrap criterion is green tally.** Not "ran to completion." Not "captured findings." Not "construction complete." This pace exists as a structural guardrail against the recurring agent failure mode where wrap is driven at "I built the thing" rather than "the thing actually works against reality." 153	# jjezs_pace ₢BBAA8
154	
155	## Character
156	
157	Design conversation requiring judgment. Mechanism choice must follow operator concurrence — load-bearing slate-time instruction.
158	
159	## Issue evidence
160	
161	Failed in BBAAX first-run gauntlet attempt at `rbtdro_onboarding_ordain_conjure` verification tail. Conjure cloud build (4m0s) and vouch (1m30s) both succeeded. All GAR artifacts confirmed via gcloud post-failure (image, attest-amd64, attest-arm64, about, vouch tagged correctly). Failure was wrest's `docker login` against `us-central1-docker.pkg.dev`:
162	
163	    Error response from daemon: Get "https://us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/v2/":
164	    net/http: request canceled while waiting for connection
165	    (Client.Timeout exceeded while awaiting headers)
166	
167	Endpoint probed clean post-failure (curl 134ms, 401 as expected; gcloud listing fast). Documented docker daemon transient per moby/moby#44350: non-configurable Client.Timeout fires before the registry auth backend responds; industry remediation across the corpus is retry. Same case had 4 curl rc=28 warnings during the preceding vouch poll, absorbed by the existing 3-consecutive tolerance.
168	
169	## Asymmetry to close
170	
171	`Tools/rbk/rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` poll loop tolerates this transient class with a 3-consecutive policy (BCG-clean, in production).
172	`Tools/rbk/rbfl_FoundryLedger.sh` `rbfl_wrest`'s docker login is single-shot, fatal on the same transient class. Same daemon, same registry boundary, different fate.
173	
174	## Scope
175	
176	Retry shape for transient-vulnerable docker daemon ↔ registry single-shot calls. Immediate surface is wrest's docker login; design surface should include other single-shot daemon calls crossing the same boundary (login, pull, push, inspect with registry round-trip).
177	
178	## Discuss before implementing — load-bearing
179	
180	Mount agent surfaces the design space and proposes a recommended shape; operator concurrence precedes landing. No pre-baking at slate-time. Design questions to seat at the table belong in the conversation, not the docket.
181	
182	## Out of scope
183	
184	- Curl/gcloud transient audit beyond the docker daemon path — distinct class
185	- Build-poll loop changes (already tolerant)
186	- Generic retry framework — only what the surveyed surface requires
187	
188	## References
189	
190	- moby/moby#44350 — non-configurable docker daemon Client.Timeout
191	- BBAAX inline-fix commits 24e24651 (depot Cloud Build URL) and dd5893ba (Mid-execution posture rule) — first-run iteration arc context

file.content:
# jjezs_pace ₢BBAA8

## Character

Design conversation requiring judgment. Mechanism choice must follow operator concurrence — load-bearing slate-time instruction.

## Issue evidence

Failed in BBAAX first-run gauntlet attempt at `rbtdro_onboarding_ordain_conjure` verification tail. Conjure cloud build (4m0s) and vouch (1m30s) both succeeded. All GAR artifacts confirmed via gcloud post-failure (image, attest-amd64, attest-arm64, about, vouch tagged correctly). Failure was wrest's `docker login` against `us-central1-docker.pkg.dev`:

    Error response from daemon: Get "https://us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/v2/":
    net/http: request canceled while waiting for connection
    (Client.Timeout exceeded while awaiting headers)

Endpoint probed clean post-failure (curl 134ms, 401 as expected; gcloud listing fast). Documented docker daemon transient per moby/moby#44350: non-configurable Client.Timeout fires before the registry auth backend responds; industry remediation across the corpus is retry. Same case had 4 curl rc=28 warnings during the preceding vouch poll, absorbed by the existing 3-consecutive tolerance.

## Asymmetry to close

`Tools/rbk/rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` poll loop tolerates this transient class with a 3-consecutive policy (BCG-clean, in production).
`Tools/rbk/rbfl_FoundryLedger.sh` `rbfl_wrest`'s docker login is single-shot, fatal on the same transient class. Same daemon, same registry boundary, different fate.

## Scope

Retry shape for transient-vulnerable docker daemon ↔ registry single-shot calls. Immediate surface is wrest's docker login; design surface should include other single-shot daemon calls crossing the same boundary (login, pull, push, inspect with registry round-trip).

## Discuss before implementing — load-bearing

Mount agent surfaces the design space and proposes a recommended shape; operator concurrence precedes landing. No pre-baking at slate-time. Design questions to seat at the table belong in the conversation, not the docket.

## Out of scope

- Curl/gcloud transient audit beyond the docker daemon path — distinct class
- Build-poll loop changes (already tolerant)
- Generic retry framework — only what the surveyed surface requires

## References

- moby/moby#44350 — non-configurable docker daemon Client.Timeout
- BBAAX inline-fix commits 24e24651 (depot Cloud Build URL) and dd5893ba (Mid-execution posture rule) — first-run iteration arc context

## Groom — ₣BB rbk-mvp-release-qualification (57/68, 11 paces remaining)

Key finding from the dockets: **the first-run (₢BBAAX) has already been attempted and got *far*** — conjure cloud build + vouch both succeeded, all GAR artifacts confirmed — before dying on a `docker login` daemon transient at the wrest step. So the heat isn't blocked on "never ran"; it's blocked on *re-running*, and the partial run has already spawned cloud-free follow-up fixes. That reframes what's worth doing while you're quota-starved.

### The two quota-blocked paces (set these aside)

| Pace | What | Why blocked |
|------|------|-------------|
| ₢BBAAX (rbw-tP-first-run) | The live gauntlet run | 2 fresh GCP projects/attempt |
| ₢BBABA (rbw-tP-branched-rerun) | Validation re-run on a coronet branch | same — 2 fresh projects |

### What's doable *now* with existing depot / no fresh projects

**Tier A — directly de-risks the next live run (highest leverage):** these are the transients that broke or threatened the first attempt. Land them and the next ₢BBAAX (when quota returns) is far likelier to go green.
- **₢BBAA8 (docker-login-retry-shape)** — design + code. Add retry to `rbfl_wrest`'s single-shot `docker login`, closing the asymmetry with `rbfc`'s already-tolerant poll loop. *This is the exact failure that killed the first run.* Marked "discuss before implementing."
- **₢BBABL (account-state-invalid-tolerance)** — memo-locked code repair. Absorb transient `401`/`ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID` in `rbfc` build-poll + `rbgo` token mint. Shape already pinned by `memo-20260527`.

**Tier B — self-contained infra, high value, no cloud:**
- **₢BBABG (fast-qualify-shellcheck-fold)** — fold shellcheck into the fast tier, parallelize via `xargs -P`, baseline-to-zero, wire as an early gate into marshal-zero. Purely local; tightens the MZ entry contract the whole heat depends on.

**Tier C — docs/ceremony, no cloud:**
- **₢BBABM (prep-release-slash-command)** — *foot-gun fix*: `/rbk-prep-release` would fail today at Step 8 (copies a retired file). Mechanical rewrite + verification sweep, 5 tracks. (Track 4 touches the git remote — needs your confirm, no project.)
- **₢BBAAH (prep-release-integration)** — wire pristine-pass precondition into prep-release. *Soft-blocked*: the artifact-read/refuse machinery can be built now, but the "after a green run" path can't be end-to-end verified without an actual green ₢BBAAX. Overlaps BBABM (both edit `rbk-prep-release.md`).
- **₢BBAAI (release-doc-revision)** — cold re-read of RELEASE.md, gauntlet vocabulary sweep, document the no-teardown + post-success cleanup ceremony.

**Tier D — read-only audit/spec, ideal for cloud-blocked time:**
- **₢BBABN (foundry-and-cloud-step-trust-audit)** — large research pace producing a ranked findings report over the `rbf*` family + Cloud Build step scripts. Zero code, zero cloud — pure analysis. Remediation deferred to operator-elected follow-ups.
- **₢BBAAq (rbs0-sync-sweep)** — reconcile RBS0/veiled specs to behavior landed across BB. Tail-of-heat reflective pace — best done *after* the other code paces land.

**Tier E — cross-heat dependency:**
- **₢BBAAx (handbook-render-imprint-coverage)** — BUK design call on imprint-channel colophons in host-agnostic handbooks. Doable, but waits on ₢A6AAn / ₢A-AAS in other heats.

---

My read: if the aim is to make the eventual live run *count*, **Tier A first** (BBAA8 + BBABL) — they're the literal transients that broke it. If you'd rather bank guaranteed-green local progress, **BBABG** is clean and self-contained, and **BBABM** removes an active foot-gun. Which do you want to mount?

Only **₢BBAAX** and **₢BBABA** create/delete depots — both need fresh GCP projects. Every other pace is depot-free. So the rail is: sink those two to the end, preserving their relative order.

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  Design conversation requiring judgment. Mechanism choice must follow operator concurrence — load-bearing slate-time instruction.
  
  ## Issue evidence
  
  Failed in BBAAX first-run gauntlet attempt at `rbtdro_onboarding_ordain_conjure` verification tail. Conjure cloud build (4m0s) and vouch (1m30s) both succeeded. All GAR artifacts confirmed via gcloud post-failure (image, attest-amd64, attest-arm64, about, vouch tagged correctly). Failure was wrest's `docker login` against `us-central1-docker.pkg.dev`:
  
      Error response from daemon: Get "https://us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/v2/":
      net/http: request canceled while waiting for connection
      (Client.Timeout exceeded while awaiting headers)
  
  Endpoint probed clean post-failure (curl 134ms, 401 as expected; gcloud listing fast). Documented docker daemon transient per moby/moby#44350: non-configurable Client.Timeout fires before the registry auth backend responds; industry remediation across the corpus is retry. Same case had 4 curl rc=28 warnings during the preceding vouch poll, absorbed by the existing 3-consecutive tolerance.
  
  ## Asymmetry to close
  
  `Tools/rbk/rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` poll loop tolerates this transient class with a 3-consecutive policy (BCG-clean, in production).
  `Tools/rbk/rbfl_FoundryLedger.sh` `rbfl_wrest`'s docker login is single-shot, fatal on the same transient class. Same daemon, same registry boundary, different fate.
  
  ## Scope
  
  Retry shape for transient-vulnerable docker daemon ↔ registry single-shot calls. Immediate surface is wrest's docker login; design surface should include other single-shot daemon calls crossing the same boundary (login, pull, push, inspect with registry round-trip).
  
  ## Discuss before implementing — load-bearing
  
  Mount agent surfaces the design space and proposes a recommended shape; operator concurrence precedes landing. No pre-baking at slate-time. Design questions to seat at the table belong in the conversation, not the docket.
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - Curl/gcloud transient audit beyond the docker daemon path — distinct class
  - Build-poll loop changes (already tolerant)
  - Generic retry framework — only what the surveyed surface requires
  
  ## References
  
  - moby/moby#44350 — non-configurable docker daemon Client.Timeout
  - BBAAX inline-fix commits 24e24651 (depot Cloud Build URL) and dd5893ba (Mid-execution posture rule) — first-run iteration arc context

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  Design conversation requiring judgment. Mechanism choice must follow operator concurrence — load-bearing slate-time instruction.
  
  ## Issue evidence
  
  Failed in BBAAX first-run gauntlet attempt at `rbtdro_onboarding_ordain_conjure` verification tail. Conjure cloud build (4m0s) and vouch (1m30s) both succeeded. All GAR artifacts confirmed via gcloud post-failure (image, attest-amd64, attest-arm64, about, vouch tagged correctly). Failure was wrest's `docker login` against `us-central1-docker.pkg.dev`:
  
      Error response from daemon: Get "https://us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/v2/":
      net/http: request canceled while waiting for connection
      (Client.Timeout exceeded while awaiting headers)
  
  Endpoint probed clean post-failure (curl 134ms, 401 as expected; gcloud listing fast). Documented docker daemon transient per moby/moby#44350: non-configurable Client.Timeout fires before the registry auth backend responds; industry remediation across the corpus is retry. Same case had 4 curl rc=28 warnings during the preceding vouch poll, absorbed by the existing 3-consecutive tolerance.
  
  ## Asymmetry to close
  
  `Tools/rbk/rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` poll loop tolerates this transient class with a 3-consecutive policy (BCG-clean, in production).
  `Tools/rbk/rbfl_FoundryLedger.sh` `rbfl_wrest`'s docker login is single-shot, fatal on the same transient class. Same daemon, same registry boundary, different fate.
  
  ## Scope
  
  Retry shape for transient-vulnerable docker daemon ↔ registry single-shot calls. Immediate surface is wrest's docker login; design surface should include other single-shot daemon calls crossing the same boundary (login, pull, push, inspect with registry round-trip).
  
  ## Discuss before implementing — load-bearing
  
  Mount agent surfaces the design space and proposes a recommended shape; operator concurrence precedes landing. No pre-baking at slate-time. Design questions to seat at the table belong in the conversation, not the docket.
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - Curl/gcloud transient audit beyond the docker daemon path — distinct class
  - Build-poll loop changes (already tolerant)
  - Generic retry framework — only what the surveyed surface requires
  
  ## References
  
  - moby/moby#44350 — non-configurable docker daemon Client.Timeout
  - BBAAX inline-fix commits 24e24651 (depot Cloud Build URL) and dd5893ba (Mid-execution posture rule) — first-run iteration arc context

efe43240b  ₢BBABP    Emit a periodic 'running <case> (Ns elapsed)' heartbeat to the console while a theurge case blocks, so the operator sees progress during the silent cloud-build poll window. New rbtdre_run_with_heartbeat helper runs the case func on the calling thread (preserving the rbtdrc thread-local context) while a background thread ticks every 30s via mpsc::recv_timeout — exits instantly when the case finishes, so short cases pay no latency. Routed both run_cases and run_single_case through it.

attachment.snippet:
1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BB
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	Release qualification gap surfaced by the ₣A_ depot-regen heat. Every existing test tier (`rbw-tf` fast, `rbw-tr` release) tolerates accumulated depot state, so silent first-build assumptions slip through. Recent spooks — kludge-aware-charge-prereq (k-prefixed hallmarks at GAR), ZRBOB_PROJECT (compose project name without runtime prefix), rbob_charged_predicate prefix omission, reliquary integrity-broken on negative test — all share a property: they only manifest on first-build paths, and none were caught until live verification surfaced them.
6	
7	This heat constructs `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine — a third tier that **refuses to run** unless marshal-zero state was just committed. The refusal is enforced by the test itself, not by ceremony or operator discipline.
8	
9	## Single operator prerequisite chain
10	
11	Operator has exactly one prerequisite chain: confirm Payor health → marshal-zero → commit → run `rbw-tP`. Payor OAuth is the only credential the operator must have ready; everything else (governor, retriever, director SAs and their RBRA files) is **minted by the qualification itself**, not restored from backup.
12	
13	## Entry contract — load-bearing
14	
15	`rbw-tP` fails-fast unless ALL of the following hold:
16	
17	- Working tree clean
18	- HEAD commit is a marshal-zero commit (detectable signature baked into `rblm_zero` per BBAAB)
19	- RBRR fields are blank (prefixes, depot project ID, etc.)
20	- No RBRA credential files present (governor, retriever, director — all deleted by marshal-zero, recreated by the qualification)
21	- No hallmark fields populated in nameplates
22	- No depot-scoped fields populated in vessel rbrv.env files
23	
24	Operator cannot skip the prerequisite. This is the property that makes the tier catch the silent-first-build bug class by construction.
25	
26	## Failure mode contract
27	
28	Mid-qualification failure means start-over-from-zero, not patch-and-continue. Documented explicitly in runbook to prevent the very accumulated-state bug class this tier exists to catch.
29	
30	## Release-branch execution contract
31	
32	Three properties bind every fixture and phase in this tier.
33	
34	**Run on a release branch.** The release machinery is engineered for branch execution, not main. The branch will accumulate many commits across a successful pristine run. After a failed run, the branch holds bygone commits that `rbw-MZ` does not touch — they remain as history; the next run starts from a fresh marshal-zero commit on top.
35	
36	**Commits during the run are first-class.** Each step that mutates regime state (rbrr.env, rbra files, vessel rbrv.env, etc.) commits the change. Container-recipe work has a documented cognitive failure mode: humans lose track of where they are in long sequences. The growing commit trail is the operator's mental anchor against that.
37	
38	**Stop on very first failure, cleanly and clearly.** A step fails → fixture stops → operator stops → debugging happens immediately on the failed branch. Stderr names the failed step; fixture exits non-zero. Recovery is `rbw-MZ` + retry on a fresh marshal-zero. No graceful degradation, no recovery branches.
39	
40	Engineering scaffolding for any other shape — partial-run cleanup, soft-delete tolerance, multi-mode failure handling, recovery-time orphan inspection, diagnostic-redundancy — is non-load-bearing and does not belong in pristine-tier code. The single mechanical path is the entire surface.
41	
42	## Tier layering
43	
44	Three tiers, escalating cost:
45	
46	| Colophon | Frontispiece | Cost | Entry contract |
47	|----------|--------------|------|----------------|
48	| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | seconds | none |
49	| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | minutes | none (accumulated state OK) |
50	| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine *(new)* | ~1 hour + cloud $ | marshal-zero just committed |
51	
52	`rbw-tP` is THE release gate. `rbw-tr` becomes the pre-pristine smoke test — cheap-and-frequent for development confidence; pristine for actual release.
53	
54	## Gauntlet test suite — design
55	
56	`rbw-tP` exec's the **gauntlet suite** — a TestSuite (not a custom orchestrator script) composed of fixtures sequenced from marshal-zero state through canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. The TestSuite mechanism is existing infrastructure (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.{fast,service,crucible,complete}.sh`); the gauntlet suite extends it.
57	
58	**Suite-of-fixtures, not orchestrator-script.** The original construction plan (custom bash orchestrator with inline tabtarget calls) was abandoned in favor of suite composition. Each section of the release-qualification sequence becomes a fixture; the suite composes them in order.
59	
60	**Naming hygiene.** "Gauntlet" is the suite name; "pristine" is reserved for the §1 pristine-lifecycle fixture and informally for tier vocabulary. The suite name does not appear at fixture or case level — distinct stratification across naming layers (suite ≠ fixture ≠ case).
61	
62	**State ladder, fixtures along it.** The suite walks a state ladder no single fixture covers:
63	
64	1. enrollment-validation (preflight, state-indifferent)
65	2. pristine-lifecycle (§1: marshal-zero gate + throwaway depot/SA lifecycle)
66	3. canonical-establish (§2: canonical depot levy + governor mantle + retriever invest + director invest + IAM propagation wait)
67	4. canonical-onboarding-sequence (§3: reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain — handbook-driven build walks per BBAAf)
68	5. regime-validation, regime-smoke (post-§3, regimes now populated)
69	6. four-mode (cloud-build mode coverage)
70	7. tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml (§4: per-crucible test fixtures, vessel-exists preconditions per BBAAs)
71	
72	No automatic teardown — see "No-teardown decision" below.
73	
74	## Dual purpose: handbook-regression coverage
75	
76	The gauntlet's primary purpose is release qualification (catch silent first-build assumptions). Its **secondary purpose** is automated handbook-regression coverage: ensure operator-facing onboarding handbook tracks (`rbh*`) stay in sync with working code so future operators following the prescribed steps aren't surprised by drift.
77	
78	Build-side handbook coverage lives in BBAAf (canonical-onboarding-sequence): each ordain case walks the corresponding director-mode handbook track (`rbw-Odf` conjure / `rbw-Oda` airgap / `rbw-Odb` bind / `rbw-Odg` graft) rather than calling inscribe/ordain machinery directly.
79	
80	**Build/run split — load-bearing.** Handbook walks cover the BUILD step (vessel construction) and stop when the hallmark lands in GAR. Run-side fixtures (per-crucible: tadmor/moriah/srjcl/pluml) stay as direct test fixtures with vessel-exists preconditions, NOT handbook walks. The split keeps run-side reusable against any vessel source — coupling them to handbook prose breaks reusability.
81	
82	Drift between fixtures and handbooks is accepted future maintenance: whoever changes one is responsible for updating the other; the gauntlet's job is to surface drift, not prevent it by construction.
83	
84	Run-side handbook coverage (`rbw-Ots`, the rack portion of `rbw-Ofc`) is explicitly out of scope for this heat.
85	
86	## Fixture disposition — Independent vs StateProgressing
87	
88	Each fixture declares a disposition flag in Rust, controlling engine behavior:
89	
90	- **Independent** — cases are self-contained; suite-order is informational. Engine permits keep-going mode for surveying.
91	- **StateProgressing** — case N's success establishes preconditions for case N+1. Engine refuses keep-going mode (incoherent: failed case leaves broken precondition). Fail-fast required.
92	
93	**Default is Independent.** Existing fixtures (`enrollment-validation`, `regime-validation`, `regime-smoke`, `four-mode`, `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml`) are Independent and require no tagging change. Only fixtures whose case-N success establishes case-N+1 preconditions get StateProgressing — currently `pristine-lifecycle`, `canonical-establish`, and `canonical-onboarding-sequence`.
94	

... [97 lines truncated] ...

(Three reorder commits landed: `ec041daf`, `f423cbd3`, `40a38876` — the first sent BBAAX to last, then I re-anchored both relative to the open set so they sit BBAAX → BBABA at the tail.)

---

## Why I went to the database — your question

You're right, and it's worth being precise about *what* I did, because part of it was fine and part wasn't.

**The sanctioned part:** I did call the JJK tools — `jjx_orient` and `jjx_show {detail: true}`. Those write `gazette_out.md`, and reading that exchange file is exactly the prescribed workflow. No problem there.

**The part you're rightly unhappy about:** when `jjx_show` returned ~50KB, the harness auto-persisted the result to its *internal* tool-result cache (`.claude/projects/.../tool-results/toolu_*.json`), and instead of staying inside the JJK interface, I reached into that cache and **python-parsed the raw JSON**, plus grep'd a persisted `cat` dump. That's the "straight to the database" move — I bypassed the abstraction and scraped harness-internal storage.

**Why I did it:** plain expedience. All 11 dockets were sitting in one big blob "right there," so I scraped it in one sweep rather than making the clean per-pace calls. The JJK-native path was `jjx_brief {coronet}` for individual dockets (or just reading `gazette_out.md` directly without the python detour). I optimized for fewer round-trips over staying inside the interface.

**Why it actually matters** (not just aesthetics): the internal tool-result JSON and the gallops store are undocumented harness internals — their shape can change, and parsing them couples my work to formats I'm not supposed to depend on. More importantly, the JJK server is the thing that enforces consistency, locking, and the officium/gazette contract; routing around it to read raw storage is exactly the discipline that the MCP-tool boundary exists to prevent. Same principle as "never read the regime files directly, go through the CLI."

Going forward for grooms: I'll use `jjx_brief` per pace (or read `gazette_out.md` as the one sanctioned file) rather than scraping the persisted blobs — even when it costs more calls.

Want me to mount ₢BBAA8 now, or keep grooming?

message.content: I wasn't shaming you, but I do want to explore when we have a 'spook' if my context preparation for you isn't good enough for the 'right way' to be obvious.  Do you see how I could have done better with either markdown explanation of available commands or printouts from job jockey?

content.text:
Yes — and I think the sharpest version of the answer is a specific pattern, not just "add more docs."

## Where the context genuinely fell short

**1. The granular commands are named but not explained.** In the MCP Command Reference, the entries that would have made the clean path obvious are listed with *zero* semantics:

```
jjx_brief      {coronet}
jjx_coronets   {firemark, remaining?, rough?}
```

Compare those to `jjx_show`, which gets a whole "Key points" block. So when I needed all 11 dockets, `jjx_show {detail}` was the command I *understood*, and it dumped a 50KB blob. `jjx_brief` — the right tool for "one docket, cleanly" — was just a name in a table; I had no model of what it returned, so I didn't reach for it. A one-line gloss on each (`jjx_brief — full docket for ONE pace; the clean way to read a single docket without the whole-heat gazette`) would have made the per-pace path the *cheap* path.

**2. Nothing anticipates the large-output failure mode.** The Groom Protocol says "Read `gazette_out.md` for full paddock and pace docket content" — it assumes the gazette reads in one go. It doesn't say what happens when the heat is big, the output overflows, and the harness auto-persists it to its internal cache. So at the exact moment I hit the fork, the doc gave no steer, and the persisted blob was sitting right there. The sanctioned answer ("re-query narrower with `jjx_brief`; never parse the persisted result files") simply isn't written down.

## The pattern I'd actually bet on

Here's the thing I noticed about myself: I am *extremely* reliable about the prohibitions written as hard "**NEVER X**" rules — I never `cd`, never `tee | tail`, never `git reset`. Those land because they're absolute, named, and carry a rationale. 

The data-access discipline for JJK was present only as a *generalizable principle* ("go through the interface" — which saturates the tabtarget and regime sections). I had enough to infer it. But inference-under-expedience is exactly where I slipped, whereas an explicit NEVER-rule would have stopped me cold.

So the single highest-leverage edit isn't more prose — it's a peer to your existing prohibitions. Something like:

> **Never read JJK's gallops store or the harness's persisted tool-result cache directly — always go through `jjx_*` commands.** When a groom's gazette overflows, re-query per-pace with `jjx_brief`, don't scrape the blob. (Same discipline as "never read regime files directly.")

That format demonstrably works on me; the implied-principle format demonstrably didn't, this once.

## Honest counterweight

I don't want to lay it all on the context. The *principle* was there and it generalizes cleanly from rules I follow without fail — and reading `gazette_out.md` directly (which I even did, partially) was always available, so I never actually *needed* the python scrape. Better docs would have made the right way obvious; I also could have applied a principle I already had. It's both.

---

This is a real spook (your word). Want me to:
- **slate it as a pace** capturing the lesson, and/or
- **make the two doc edits now** — gloss `jjx_brief`/`jjx_coronets` in the command reference, and add the NEVER-rule to the JJK section of CLAUDE.md?

# JJK Kit Source

Edits here apply to the kit source only. Do not modify the active installation in `.claude/` or the parent project's CLAUDE.md.

lastPrompt: Would you look at the outputs and/ or the job jockey source/ specs itself to get really clear on the way the commands work now?  That might influence the way we improve the markdown.  We might even co…

content.text: Now the picture sharpens. `jjx_coronets` outputs **only coronet IDs, one per line** (in heat order, with `--remaining`/`--rough` filters) — no docket, no silks. So it can't show docket bodies. The interesting one is `jjx_brief`. Let me read its handler and the orient/show emit paths.

lastPrompt: Would you look at the outputs and/ or the job jockey source/ specs itself to get really clear on the way the commands work now?  That might influence the way we improve the markdown.  We might even co…

content.text:
I've now read the source and specs. Here's the accurate model — and it changes the recommendation.

## Ground truth: what these commands actually do

| Command | Routes to | Output | Channel |
|---------|-----------|--------|---------|
| `jjx_brief {coronet}` | `get_spec` | **`tacks[0].text`** — raw docket for ONE pace, "no decoration, no prefix" | **inline** (tool result) |
| `jjx_coronets {firemark, remaining?, rough?}` | `get_coronets` | **coronet IDs only**, one per line, in heat order, "no silks" | **inline** |
| `jjx_show {detail}` / `jjx_orient` | parade / saddle | paddock + per-pace dockets | **gazette file** (`gazette_out.md`); inline result is just the tables |

So the design is deliberate and clean: **single docket → `jjx_brief`, inline, tiny. Whole-heat dump → gazette file.** The right move for "read the 11 dockets" was either loop `jjx_brief` per coronet, or read `gazette_out.md` (the file). Parsing the harness's persisted tool-result JSON was never on the sanctioned map — that path simply doesn't exist in the design; I invented it.

## So — could your context have made this obvious? Yes, two concrete gaps

**1. The glosses are missing, and the source hands them to us verbatim.** In `Tools/jjk/claude-jjk-core.md` the reference table has bare signatures:
```
jjx_brief      {coronet}
jjx_coronets   {firemark, remaining?, rough?}
```
The specs (`JJSCGS`, `JJSCGC`) already contain crisp one-liners I could paste:
- **brief** — "raw docket text for one pace, inline, no decoration"
- **coronets** — "coronet IDs for a heat in order, one per line, no silks"

Had those been in the table, `jjx_brief` is the obvious tool and I never reach for a blob.

**2. The failure-mode steer is absent.** The "Gazette output" note says show/orient "write gazette_out.md — read the gazette file." It never says *brief returns inline, prefer it for single dockets, and never scrape the persisted tool-result cache when a gazette is large.* That's the exact fork I hit.

## The real ergonomic gap — and a command worth adjusting

Here's what actually nudged me toward the blob, now that I can see it: **to build a groom overview (silks + state + docket per pace) there is no cheap command.** `jjx_coronets` gives IDs with *no silks/state*; `jjx_brief` gives docket with *no silks/state*. So the only one-shot "show me everything about every pace" is `jjx_show {detail}` — which is precisely the heavy gazette dump. The lightweight middle path doesn't exist.

The smallest high-leverage fix: **give `jjx_coronets` a table mode** — one line per pace, `₢coronet  state  silks`, still inline, still no gazette. That single call becomes the groom skeleton; then `jjx_brief` per pace for the ones you want to read deeply. Heavy `show {detail}` stays for when you genuinely want the whole heat written to a file. It composes: cheap-skeleton → cheap-depth, blob never needed.

(That's a Rust change in `jjrgc_get_coronets.rs` + `JJSCGC` spec + the doc table, rebuilt via `vow-b` — your architecture call.)

---

How do you want to proceed? Options, roughly increasing in scope:

1. **Doc-only** — add the brief/coronets glosses + a "never scrape persisted results; prefer brief / read gazette_out.md" rule to `claude-jjk-core.md`.
2. **Doc + command** — also add the `jjx_coronets` table mode (Rust + spec + rebuild).
3. **Capture as a spook pace** in ₣BB (or wherever JJK ergonomics live) and let it be scheduled rather than done inline.

My lean: **1 now** (cheap, immediately useful), and **slate 2 as a pace** so the command change gets proper design + rebuild + test attention rather than being rushed mid-conversation. Which way?

message.content: Would you remind me what the 'groom' keyword causes?  specific jjx commands, that is

content.text:
Per the **Groom Protocol** in `claude-jjk-core.md`, "groom" triggers exactly **one** jjx command:

```
jjx_show {target: FIREMARK, detail: true, remaining: true}
```

…followed by reading `gazette_out.md` for the paddock + per-pace docket content. The remaining steps are non-tool: display the overview, then enter planning mode (where *you* might then call `jjx_enroll`/`jjx_reorder`/`jjx_redocket`/`jjx_paddock`, but those are driven by the planning conversation, not by the "groom" keyword itself).

The full protocol:
1. `jjx_show {target: FIREMARK, detail: true, remaining: true}`
2. Read `gazette_out.md`
3. Display overview (silks, progress, remaining paces w/ dockets)
4. Planning mode (suggest slate / rail / reslate / paddock ops)

Worth flagging, given our whole thread: **the prescribed groom command is exactly the heavy `detail: true` gazette dump** — the one that produced the 50KB blob. So the protocol itself steers toward the bulky path; on a large heat like ₣BB (68 paces) that gazette overflows. That's an argument that the groom-ergonomics fix isn't just doc glosses — the protocol could itself be rewritten to use the cheap skeleton (`jjx_coronets` table mode, once it exists) for the overview and reserve full dockets for `jjx_brief` on demand. Which is the command-change path #2 from my last message.

message.content: Was the text large because it is showing you old paces or old tacks?

content.text: Let me check the source rather than guess — specifically whether parade honors `remaining` and whether it emits one tack or the full tack history.

lastPrompt: Was the text large because it is showing you old paces or old tacks?

content.text:
Neither — I checked the parade source, and both of those are explicitly filtered out. The size was genuine, current content.

**Not old paces.** The groom uses `remaining: true`, and the heat-detail loop skips them: `if args.remaining && (tack.state == Complete || tack.state == Abandoned) { continue; }` (jjrpd_parade.rs:220, and again in the gazette-population pass at :396). So the 57 closed paces contributed nothing — only the ~11 rough/bridled ones emitted dockets.

**Not old tacks.** In the *heat* view, each pace emits only `pace.tacks.first()` → the current docket (`tack.text`, line 218/226). The full tack history (`pace.tacks.iter().rev()`, the reslate chain) is emitted **only in the single-pace view** (lines 132–153), never in a heat groom. So no stale docket revisions are pulled in.

**So why 50KB?** The 11 *live* dockets are simply big. ₣BB's remaining paces carry heavyweight specs — BBABN (trust-audit) is ~120 lines with tables, BBABM (prep-release) ~90 lines across 5 tracks, plus BBAAX, BBAAq, etc. Eleven current dockets of that size ≈ 50KB.

**One extra finding worth your attention:** in heat-detail mode the dockets are emitted **twice** — once to the inline tool result (lines 216–234) *and* again into the gazette (lines 391–402). (The paddock goes only to the gazette.) So `jjx_show {detail}` on a big heat double-renders every docket — that's part of why the *inline* result overflowed and got auto-persisted in the first place, independent of the gazette. That duplication looks non-load-bearing: the inline result already carries everything the gazette docket notices do.

This sharpens the earlier conclusion: the bloat is intrinsic to "dump all current dockets at once," which is exactly what the groom protocol asks for. The `jjx_coronets` table-mode fix sidesteps it because a groom *overview* rarely needs all 11 full bodies — skeleton first, then `jjx_brief` the ones you'll actually work. And the docket double-emit is a separate, smaller cleanup candidate in `jjrpd_parade.rs`.

message.content: There are seemingly multiple simple fixes we can do, including that double docket printout.  What ones do you suggest?

content.text:
Here's my ranked list, split by cost — because the cheap ones eliminate the actual pain *today* without a rebuild, and the Rust ones carry a small design fork worth doing deliberately.

## Do now — doc-only, no build, kills the blob immediately

**1. Rewrite the Groom Protocol to not prescribe `detail: true`.** This is the single highest-leverage fix and it's pure markdown. The protocol currently mandates the exact blob-producing call. Change it to:
- `jjx_show {target, remaining: true}` (no `detail`) → cheap inline table (No/State/Pace/Coronet + "Next" callout), no docket bodies, no gazette overflow.
- then `jjx_brief {coronet}` on the specific paces you'll actually work.

That alone removes both the 50KB gazette *and* the double-emit exposure from every future groom — without touching Rust.

**2. Gloss `jjx_brief` and `jjx_coronets` in the command reference** (`claude-jjk-core.md`), liftable verbatim from the specs:
- `jjx_brief {coronet}` — raw docket text for ONE pace, inline, no decoration. The clean single-docket read.
- `jjx_coronets {firemark, remaining?, rough?}` — coronet IDs in heat order, one per line, no silks.

**3. Add the NEVER-rule** (mirrors your existing `cd` / `tee|tail` / `git reset` prohibitions, the format that reliably lands on me): *never parse the harness's persisted tool-result cache or the gallops JSON directly — read dockets via `jjx_brief`, or read `gazette_out.md` when a gazette was written.*

## Slate as a pace — Rust, needs `vow-b` rebuild + test + spec touch

**4. Remove the double docket emit in `jjrpd_parade.rs` (heat-detail).** Real, but there's a fork to decide, not pre-bake: the inline body (lines ~224–231) and the gazette body (lines ~391–402) both carry `tack.text`. The gazette is the *designed* docket channel, so the inline copy is the redundant one — **but** stripping it changes what a direct CLI `jjx_show BB --detail` prints to stdout (the gazette is only emitted at the MCP layer). So the design call is "inline keeps the header line + table, gazette owns the bodies" vs "dedup at the MCP boundary." That judgment is why it belongs in a mounted pace, not a hallway fix.

**5. `jjx_coronets` table mode** (`₢coronet  state  silks` per line, still inline). The proper enabler for fix #1's cheap skeleton — gives silks+state in one call so a groom never needs the heavy path. Composes cleanly with `jjx_brief` for depth.

## My recommendation

Do **1–3 now** (trivial, and they fix the lived problem this turn). **Slate 4+5 together** as one JJK-ergonomics pace — and notably **not in ₣BB** (that's rbk release qualification; unrelated). **₣BD `jjk-v4-1-fast-tweaks`** looks like the natural home — small JJK improvements is literally its name.

Two things before I act:
- Want me to make edits **1–3** now to `Tools/jjk/claude-jjk-core.md`? (That's kit source — its own `CLAUDE.md` says edit the kit, not the active `.claude/` install or the root CLAUDE.md, so the edits land there and propagate on next install.)
- Should I **slate 4+5 into ₣BD**, or do you want to confirm the heat first?

I agree also on removing the 'detail' parameter but am rather inclined to make it the _default_; when we groom, I really do want the whole picture in your mind and the chats aren't long after that- it is the point of a groom to work on the whole picture.  Okay to do simple markdown changes now and slate jjk pace for code/spec changes- the lack of detail JJS*adoc documents needs consideration and repair

95	
96	All params are JSON objects. `?` = optional, `[]` = array. Booleans default to false.
97	
98	```
99	jjx_open           {}
100	jjx_show           {target?, detail?, remaining?}
101	jjx_list           {status?}
102	jjx_orient         {firemark}
103	jjx_create         {silks}
104	jjx_enroll         {firemark, before?, after?, first?, size_limit?} # silks+docket via gazette_in.md only
105	jjx_reorder        {firemark, move?, before?, after?, first?, last?}
106	jjx_alter          {firemark, racing?, stabled?, silks?}
107	jjx_record         {identity, files[], size_limit?, intent?}
108	jjx_close          {coronet, summary?, size_limit?}
109	jjx_log            {firemark, limit?}
110	jjx_search         {pattern, actionable?}
111	jjx_archive        {firemark, size_limit?}
112	jjx_transfer       {firemark, to, coronets, size_limit?}
113	jjx_continue       {firemark}
114	jjx_paddock        {firemark?, note?, size_limit?}                  # gazette_in.md to set, gazette_out.md to get
115	jjx_relocate       {coronet, to, before?, after?, first?}
116	jjx_redocket       {size_limit?}                                    # docket via gazette_in.md only; supports mass reslate
117	jjx_relabel        {coronet, silks}
118	jjx_drop           {coronet}
119	jjx_brief      {coronet}
120	jjx_coronets   {firemark, remaining?, rough?}
121	jjx_landing        {coronet, agent, content?}
122	jjx_validate       {}
123	jjx_bind           {alias, reldir}                                  # remote: create legatio session (alias resolves BURN profile)
124	jjx_send           {legatio, command}                               # remote: synchronous exec on fundus
125	jjx_plant          {legatio, commit}                                # remote: reset fundus to exact commit
126	jjx_relay          {legatio, tabtarget, timeout, firemark}          # remote: async dispatch via nohup
127	jjx_check          {pensum, timeout}                                # remote: probe/poll pensum status
128	jjx_fetch          {legatio, path}                                  # remote: read single file from fundus
129	```
130	
131	**Key points:**
132	- `jjx_show` takes firemark OR coronet in the `target` param
133	  - Heat overview: `{"target": "AF"}`
134	  - Single pace: `{"target": "AFAAb"}`
135	  - Additional params: `detail`, `remaining` only
136	- `jjx_orient` output includes next actionable pace — no separate show call needed
137	- **Gazette output**: `jjx_orient`, `jjx_show` (with detail), and `jjx_paddock` (getter) write `gazette_out.md` with paddock and pace docket notices. Read the gazette file after these commands to get full content.
138	- **Gazette input**: `jjx_enroll`, `jjx_redocket`, and `jjx_paddock` (setter) read docket/content from `gazette_in.md`. Gazette is the sole input path for docket content — no JSON param fallback.
139	- `jjx_redocket` supports **mass reslate**: multiple `# jjezs_reslate <coronet>` notices in a single `gazette_in.md`, each with its own docket body. All paces updated in one call.
140	- `jjx_paddock` `note` param: optional short string appended to the paddock discussion commit message (e.g., `{"note": "updated after spook fix"}`)
141	- `jjx_close` takes `summary` as a string param (not stdin pipe)
142	- `jjx_record` takes `files` as a native JSON array: `["file1.rs", "file2.rs"]`
143	- `jjx_transfer` takes `coronets` as a JSON-encoded string (not a native array): `"[\"AYAAA\", \"AYAAB\"]"`
144	
145	### Officium Protocol
146	
147	Each chat session must open an officium before using any jjx commands.
148	
149	1. **At chat start**, call `jjx_open` (no params, no officium field). It returns a ☉-prefixed identity string (e.g., `☉260327-1000`).
150	2. **On every subsequent jjx call**, pass the returned identity as the `officium` field on the MCP tool (sibling to `command` and `params`).
151	3. **Self-healing**: If any jjx command fails with "Officium directory not found", call `jjx_open` again to create a fresh officium, then retry.
152	
153	The ☉ (U+2609 SUN) prefix parallels ₣/₢ for firemarks/coronets. Pass it exactly as returned — the dispatcher strips it.
154	
155	Gazette file exchange uses two directional files in the officium exchange directory. Every jjx MCP call unconditionally deletes both gazette files on entry (read+delete `gazette_in.md`, delete `gazette_out.md`). Gazette content has single-MCP-call lifetime — it is a parameter or a return value, not persistent state.
156	
157	- **`gazette_in.md`** (agent → server): write before calling a setter command (`jjx_enroll`, `jjx_redocket`, `jjx_paddock` setter).
158	- **`gazette_out.md`** (server → agent): written by getter commands, read after they return. The next jjx call of any kind deletes it.
159	  - `jjx_orient` → `# jjezs_paddock <firemark>` + paddock content, `# jjezs_pace <coronet>` + docket content (for next actionable pace)
160	  - `jjx_show` (with detail) → `# jjezs_paddock <firemark>` + paddock content, `# jjezs_pace <coronet>` + docket per pace
161	  - `jjx_paddock` (getter) → `# jjezs_paddock <firemark>` + paddock content
162	
163	**Gazette wire format (setter commands):**
164	Each notice is a `#`-header line with slug and lede, followed by content body. The lede is **exactly one whitespace-free token** (silks / coronet / firemark) — nothing follows it on the `#` line; the body goes on the lines beneath. This is uniform across every input slug: `jjezs_slate` takes silks, `jjezs_reslate` takes a coronet, `jjezs_paddock` takes a firemark, and in each case appending extra text to the lede folds it into the identity and fails validation. Write `gazette_in.md`, then call the command.
165	
166	**Critical: `#` (H1) in gazette_in.md is a wire format delimiter, NOT a markdown heading.** For single-notice commands (enroll, paddock set), use exactly ONE `#` line. For mass reslate, each `# jjezs_reslate` line starts a new notice. All markdown headings within body content must use `##` or deeper — a bare `#` line inside content will be parsed as a notice boundary.
167	
168	Wrong (parsed as TWO notices, fails with `unknown slug 'Paddock:'`):
169	```

166	**Critical: `#` (H1) in gazette_in.md is a wire format delimiter, NOT a markdown heading.** For single-notice commands (enroll, paddock set), use exactly ONE `#` line. For mass reslate, each `# jjezs_reslate` line starts a new notice. All markdown headings within body content must use `##` or deeper — a bare `#` line inside content will be parsed as a notice boundary.
167	
168	Wrong (parsed as TWO notices, fails with `unknown slug 'Paddock:'`):
169	```
170	# jjezs_paddock ₣BO
171	# Paddock: rbk-11-mvp-tactical
172	body...
173	```
174	
175	Right:
176	```
177	# jjezs_paddock ₣BO
178	## Paddock: rbk-11-mvp-tactical
179	body...
180	```
181	
182	**On failure:** If a gazette setter command fails, `gazette_in.md` is already consumed (deleted on entry). You must re-write it from scratch before retrying — there is nothing to re-read.
183	
184	| Command | Write to `gazette_in.md` | Then call with params |
185	|---------|--------------------------|----------------------|
186	| `jjx_enroll` | `# jjezs_slate <silks>` + docket body | `{"firemark": "XX", "before?": ..., "after?": ..., "first?": ...}` |
187	| `jjx_redocket` | `# jjezs_reslate <coronet>` + docket body | `{}` (coronet is in gazette lede) |
188	| `jjx_redocket` (mass) | Multiple `# jjezs_reslate <coronet>` notices, each with docket body | `{}` |
189	| `jjx_paddock` (set) | `# jjezs_paddock <firemark>` + content body | `{"note?": "commit annotation"}` |
190	
191	Gazette paths: `.claude/jjm/officia/<officium-id>/gazette_in.md` and `gazette_out.md`.
192	
193	Example — reslate a pace docket:
194	1. Write `gazette_in.md`: `# jjezs_reslate AvAAH\n\n## Character\nNew docket content...`
195	2. Call: `jjx_redocket` with `{}` (coronet parsed from gazette lede)
196	
197	Example — mass reslate multiple paces:
198	1. Write `gazette_in.md` with multiple notices: `# jjezs_reslate AvAAH\n\nFirst docket...\n# jjezs_reslate AvAAI\n\nSecond docket...`
199	2. Call: `jjx_redocket` with `{}`
200	
201	**Read-modify-write workflow** (paddock editing):
202	1. Call `jjx_paddock` getter → reads `gazette_out.md`
203	2. Rename `gazette_out.md` → `gazette_in.md`, edit content
204	3. Call `jjx_paddock` setter
205	
206	### Mount Protocol
207	
208	When user says "mount" or you need to engage the next pace:
209	
210	1. Run `jjx_orient` command with the firemark. **Firemark is required** — if the user says "mount" without specifying a heat and you have no prior heat context in this session, ask which heat to mount rather than guessing. If you have prior context (previously mounted/groomed heat), use that firemark.
211	2. Parse output: Racing-heats table, Heat/Next/Docket/Recent-work sections. Read paddock and pace docket from the gazette file written to the officium exchange directory.
212	3. **Read the paddock before the docket.** The paddock tells you the shape of the work and what's been learned; the docket tells you what to do next. Orientation before action.
213	4. **Lead with the pace goal in one sentence**, distilled from the docket — what this pace is trying to accomplish, stated precisely. Paces can sit weeks between slating and mounting; the operator needs goal recall first, not heat scenery. Then display brief heat context (silks + paddock one-liner + recent work) and finally the full docket.
214	5. **Name assessment**: If pace silks doesn't fit docket, offer rename via `jjx_relabel`
215	6. Analyze docket, propose approach (2-4 bullets), assess execution strategy:
216	   - Model tier: haiku (mechanical), sonnet (standard dev), opus (architectural)
217	   - Parallelization: file independence, task decomposability
218	   - State recommendation explicitly (e.g., "Sequential sonnet — single file")
219	7. Ask to proceed, then begin work
220	
221	### Groom Protocol
222	
223	When user says "groom":
224	
225	1. Run `jjx_show` command with `{target: FIREMARK, detail: true, remaining: true}`
226	2. Read `gazette_out.md` for full paddock and pace docket content
227	3. Display overview: heat silks, progress, remaining paces with dockets (from gazette)
228	4. Enter planning mode: suggest structural operations (slate new paces, rail to reorder, reslate to refine dockets, paddock review)
229	
230	### Foray Protocol
231	
232	When user says "foray" or asks to run something on a remote machine:
233	
234	**Concepts:**
235	- **Legatio**: A standing SSH session to a remote project (fundus). Created by `jjx_bind`, identified by a token (L0, L1, ...). Persists for the officium lifetime.
236	- **Pensum**: An async dispatched job within a legatio. Created by `jjx_relay`, identified by a `₱`-prefixed token. Each pensum maps to a remote temp directory on the fundus.
237	- **Curia**: The invoking side (where Claude runs). **Fundus**: The executing side (where the tabtarget runs).
238	
239	**Fundus constants** (use these for `jjx_bind`): the default reldir and BURN alias are project-specific — get them from your project's veiled `claude-jjk-*.md` (if present) or the target's BUK Regime Node profile.
240	
241	**Workflow:**
242	
243	1. **Bind** (once per session per host): `jjx_bind` with `{alias: "<BURN_ALIAS>", reldir: "<your-project-reldir>"}`. Resolves the BURN profile on the curia to extract host/user/command, then probes via SSH. Returns legatio token.
244	2. **Ensure curia is clean and pushed**: notch if needed, `git push` if needed. `jjx_relay` will refuse dispatch if working tree is dirty or HEAD is unpushed.
245	3. **Plant**: `jjx_plant` with `{legatio: "L0", commit: "<HEAD SHA>"}`. Resets fundus to exact commit.
246	4. **Relay**: `jjx_relay` with `{legatio: "L0", tabtarget: "<filename>.sh", timeout: <seconds>, firemark: "<heat>"}`. Returns pensum token.
247	5. **Check**: `jjx_check` with `{pensum: "<token>", timeout: 0}` for instant probe, or `timeout: <seconds>` to poll until terminal. Returns BURX fields + liveness report. On terminal status, also returns file list from remote temp dir.
248	6. **Fetch** (selective): `jjx_fetch` with `{legatio: "L0", path: "<absolute-path>"}` to retrieve specific result files. Use paths from the check file list.
249	
250	**Liveness states** (from `jjx_check`):
251	- **running**: BURX_EXIT_STATUS absent, PID alive
252	- **orphaned**: BURX_EXIT_STATUS absent, PID dead (crashed without writing status)
253	- **stopped**: BURX_EXIT_STATUS present (check exit code)
254	- **lost**: burx.env file missing
255	
256	**`jjx_send`** is for synchronous one-off commands (no pensum, no polling). Use when the command is short and you need inline results.
257	
258	**Windows fundus body discipline:** When the fundus is a Windows host, both `jjx_send` and `jjx_relay`-dispatched tabtargets traverse the cmd.exe → wsl.exe / cygwin / PowerShell transport stack. Body authoring rules (escape `\$name` for w-letter wsl.exe transit, no heredocs, single-line `;`-joined for cmd.exe transit, lazy-flush avoidance for PowerShell cmdlets, etc.) live in your project's Windows scripting guide, if present. Empirical record under `Memos/memo-YYYYMMDD-windows-transport-{topic}.md`.
259	
260	### Commit Discipline
261	
262	When working on a heat, use `jjx_record` for commits with heat/pace affiliation.
263	
264	**Pace-affiliated commit** (active pace provides context):
265	Use `jjx_record` with `{identity: "CORONET", files: ["file1", "file2"], intent: "description"}`
266	
267	**Heat-affiliated commit** (no active pace, but part of heat work):
268	Use `jjx_record` with `{identity: "FIREMARK", files: ["file1", "file2"], intent: "description"}`

2. **On every subsequent jjx call**, pass the returned identity as the `officium` field on the MCP tool (sibling to `command` and `params`).
3. **Self-healing**: If any jjx command fails with "Officium directory not found", call `jjx_open` again to create a fresh officium, then retry.

The ☉ (U+2609 SUN) prefix parallels ₣/₢ for firemarks/coronets. Pass it exactly as returned — the dispatcher strips it.

Gazette file exchange uses two directional files in the officium exchange directory. Every jjx MCP call unconditionally deletes both gazette files on entry (read+delete `gazette_in.md`, delete `gazette_out.md`). Gazette content has single-MCP-call lifetime — it is a parameter or a return value, not persistent state.

- **`gazette_in.md`** (agent → server): write before calling a setter command (`jjx_enroll`, `jjx_redocket`, `jjx_paddock` setter).
- **`gazette_out.md`** (server → agent): written by getter commands, read after they return. The next jjx call of any kind deletes it.
  - `jjx_orient` → `# jjezs_paddock <firemark>` + paddock content, `# jjezs_pace <coronet>` + docket content (for next actionable pace)
  - `jjx_show` (with detail) → `# jjezs_paddock <firemark>` + paddock content, `# jjezs_pace <coronet>` + docket per pace
  - `jjx_paddock` (getter) → `# jjezs_paddock <firemark>` + paddock content

**Gazette wire format (setter commands):**
Each notice is a `#`-header line with slug and lede, followed by content body. The lede is **exactly one whitespace-free token** (silks / coronet / firemark) — nothing follows it on the `#` line; the body goes on the lines beneath. This is uniform across every input slug: `jjezs_slate` takes silks, `jjezs_reslate` takes a coronet, `jjezs_paddock` takes a firemark, and in each case appending extra text to the lede folds it into the identity and fails validation. Write `gazette_in.md`, then call the command.

**Critical: `#` (H1) in gazette_in.md is a wire format delimiter, NOT a markdown heading.** For single-notice commands (enroll, paddock set), use exactly ONE `#` line. For mass reslate, each `# jjezs_reslate` line starts a new notice. All markdown headings within body content must use `##` or deeper — a bare `#` line inside content will be parsed as a notice boundary.

Wrong (parsed as TWO notices, fails with `unknown slug 'Paddock:'`):
```
# jjezs_paddock ₣BO
# Paddock: rbk-11-mvp-tactical
body...
```

Right:
```
# jjezs_paddock ₣BO
## Paddock: rbk-11-mvp-tactical
body...
```

**On failure:** If a gazette setter command fails, `gazette_in.md` is already consumed (deleted on entry). You must re-write it from scratch before retrying — there is nothing to re-read.

| Command | Write to `gazette_in.md` | Then call with params |
|---------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| `jjx_enroll` | `# jjezs_slate <silks>` + docket body | `{"firemark": "XX", "before?": ..., "after?": ..., "first?": ...}` |
| `jjx_redocket` | `# jjezs_reslate <coronet>` + docket body | `{}` (coronet is in gazette lede) |
| `jjx_redocket` (mass) | Multiple `# jjezs_reslate <coronet>` notices, each with docket body | `{}` |
| `jjx_paddock` (set) | `# jjezs_paddock <firemark>` + content body | `{"note?": "commit annotation"}` |

Gazette paths: `.claude/jjm/officia/<officium-id>/gazette_in.md` and `gazette_out.md`.

Example — reslate a pace docket:
1. Write `gazette_in.md`: `# jjezs_reslate AvAAH\n\n## Character\nNew docket content...`
2. Call: `jjx_redocket` with `{}` (coronet parsed from gazette lede)

Example — mass reslate multiple paces:
1. Write `gazette_in.md` with multiple notices: `# jjezs_reslate AvAAH\n\nFirst docket...\n# jjezs_reslate AvAAI\n\nSecond docket...`
2. Call: `jjx_redocket` with `{}`

**Read-modify-write workflow** (paddock editing):
1. Call `jjx_paddock` getter → reads `gazette_out.md`
2. Rename `gazette_out.md` → `gazette_in.md`, edit content
3. Call `jjx_paddock` setter

### Mount Protocol

When user says "mount" or you need to engage the next pace:

1. Run `jjx_orient` command with the firemark. **Firemark is required** — if the user says "mount" without specifying a heat and you have no prior heat context in this session, ask which heat to mount rather than guessing. If you have prior context (previously mounted/groomed heat), use that firemark.
2. Parse output: Racing-heats table, Heat/Next/Docket/Recent-work sections. Read paddock and pace docket from the gazette file written to the officium exchange directory.
3. **Read the paddock before the docket.** The paddock tells you the shape of the work and what's been learned; the docket tells you what to do next. Orientation before action.
4. **Lead with the pace goal in one sentence**, distilled from the docket — what this pace is trying to accomplish, stated precisely. Paces can sit weeks between slating and mounting; the operator needs goal recall first, not heat scenery. Then display brief heat context (silks + paddock one-liner + recent work) and finally the full docket.
5. **Name assessment**: If pace silks doesn't fit docket, offer rename via `jjx_relabel`
6. Analyze docket, propose approach (2-4 bullets), assess execution strategy:
   - Model tier: haiku (mechanical), sonnet (standard dev), opus (architectural)
   - Parallelization: file independence, task decomposability
   - State recommendation explicitly (e.g., "Sequential sonnet — single file")
7. Ask to proceed, then begin work

### Groom Protocol

When user says "groom":

1. Run `jjx_show` command with `{target: FIREMARK, detail: true, remaining: true}`
2. Read `gazette_out.md` for full paddock and pace docket content
3. Display overview: heat silks, progress, remaining paces with dockets (from gazette)
4. Enter planning mode: suggest structural operations (slate new paces, rail to reorder, reslate to refine dockets, paddock review)

### Foray Protocol

When user says "foray" or asks to run something on a remote machine:

**Concepts:**
- **Legatio**: A standing SSH session to a remote project (fundus). Created by `jjx_bind`, identified by a token (L0, L1, ...). Persists for the officium lifetime.
- **Pensum**: An async dispatched job within a legatio. Created by `jjx_relay`, identified by a `₱`-prefixed token. Each pensum maps to a remote temp directory on the fundus.
- **Curia**: The invoking side (where Claude runs). **Fundus**: The executing side (where the tabtarget runs).

**Fundus constants** (use these for `jjx_bind`): the default reldir and BURN alias are project-specific — get them from your project's veiled `claude-jjk-*.md` (if present) or the target's BUK Regime Node profile.

**Workflow:**

1. **Bind** (once per session per host): `jjx_bind` with `{alias: "<BURN_ALIAS>", reldir: "<your-project-reldir>"}`. Resolves the BURN profile on the curia to extract host/user/command, then probes via SSH. Returns legatio token.
2. **Ensure curia is clean and pushed**: notch if needed, `git push` if needed. `jjx_relay` will refuse dispatch if working tree is dirty or HEAD is unpushed.
3. **Plant**: `jjx_plant` with `{legatio: "L0", commit: "<HEAD SHA>"}`. Resets fundus to exact commit.
4. **Relay**: `jjx_relay` with `{legatio: "L0", tabtarget: "<filename>.sh", timeout: <seconds>, firemark: "<heat>"}`. Returns pensum token.
5. **Check**: `jjx_check` with `{pensum: "<token>", timeout: 0}` for instant probe, or `timeout: <seconds>` to poll until terminal. Returns BURX fields + liveness report. On terminal status, also returns file list from remote temp dir.
6. **Fetch** (selective): `jjx_fetch` with `{legatio: "L0", path: "<absolute-path>"}` to retrieve specific result files. Use paths from the check file list.

**Liveness states** (from `jjx_check`):
- **running**: BURX_EXIT_STATUS absent, PID alive
- **orphaned**: BURX_EXIT_STATUS absent, PID dead (crashed without writing status)
- **stopped**: BURX_EXIT_STATUS present (check exit code)
- **lost**: burx.env file missing

**`jjx_send`** is for synchronous one-off commands (no pensum, no polling). Use when the command is short and you need inline results.

**Windows fundus body discipline:** When the fundus is a Windows host, both `jjx_send` and `jjx_relay`-dispatched tabtargets traverse the cmd.exe → wsl.exe / cygwin / PowerShell transport stack. Body authoring rules (escape `\$name` for w-letter wsl.exe transit, no heredocs, single-line `;`-joined for cmd.exe transit, lazy-flush avoidance for PowerShell cmdlets, etc.) live in your project's Windows scripting guide, if present. Empirical record under `Memos/memo-YYYYMMDD-windows-transport-{topic}.md`.

### Commit Discipline

When working on a heat, use `jjx_record` for commits with heat/pace affiliation.

**Pace-affiliated commit** (active pace provides context):
Use `jjx_record` with `{identity: "CORONET", files: ["file1", "file2"], intent: "description"}`

**Heat-affiliated commit** (no active pace, but part of heat work):
Use `jjx_record` with `{identity: "FIREMARK", files: ["file1", "file2"], intent: "description"}`

250	**Liveness states** (from `jjx_check`):
251	- **running**: BURX_EXIT_STATUS absent, PID alive
252	- **orphaned**: BURX_EXIT_STATUS absent, PID dead (crashed without writing status)
253	- **stopped**: BURX_EXIT_STATUS present (check exit code)
254	- **lost**: burx.env file missing
255	
256	**`jjx_send`** is for synchronous one-off commands (no pensum, no polling). Use when the command is short and you need inline results.
257	
258	**Windows fundus body discipline:** When the fundus is a Windows host, both `jjx_send` and `jjx_relay`-dispatched tabtargets traverse the cmd.exe → wsl.exe / cygwin / PowerShell transport stack. Body authoring rules (escape `\$name` for w-letter wsl.exe transit, no heredocs, single-line `;`-joined for cmd.exe transit, lazy-flush avoidance for PowerShell cmdlets, etc.) live in your project's Windows scripting guide, if present. Empirical record under `Memos/memo-YYYYMMDD-windows-transport-{topic}.md`.
259	
260	### Commit Discipline
261	
262	When working on a heat, use `jjx_record` for commits with heat/pace affiliation.
263	
264	**Pace-affiliated commit** (active pace provides context):
265	Use `jjx_record` with `{identity: "CORONET", files: ["file1", "file2"], intent: "description"}`
266	
267	**Heat-affiliated commit** (no active pace, but part of heat work):
268	Use `jjx_record` with `{identity: "FIREMARK", files: ["file1", "file2"], intent: "description"}`
269	
270	Synthesize intent from the conversation — describe *what* was accomplished, not *how*.
271	
272	**Size guard — ALL commands accepting `size_limit?` (record, close, archive, paddock, enroll, redocket, transfer)**: If ANY jjx command fails due to size limits, STOP. Report the byte count, limit, and per-file breakdown to the user, then WAIT for the user's review. The guard is a byte-sanity review step — its purpose is catching unintended bulk (e.g., a binary that snuck in), not gating decomposition. The default is uniform 50KB across all seven commands. Expected outcome: the user confirms the bytes are legitimate and directs you to raise the limit. Do NOT offer splitting as a parallel option — splitting enters the picture only if the user explicitly raises it, because the work genuinely decomposes. NEVER auto-override `size_limit`.
273

275	
276	**Multi-Officium Discipline:**
277	Multiple Claude officia (concurrent chat sessions, each with its own ☉-prefixed officium ID) may work concurrently in the same repo. The explicit file list in `jjx_record` enables orthogonal commits.
278	
279	- Claude is **additive only** — make commits, never discard changes

file.content:
2. **On every subsequent jjx call**, pass the returned identity as the `officium` field on the MCP tool (sibling to `command` and `params`).
3. **Self-healing**: If any jjx command fails with "Officium directory not found", call `jjx_open` again to create a fresh officium, then retry.

The ☉ (U+2609 SUN) prefix parallels ₣/₢ for firemarks/coronets. Pass it exactly as returned — the dispatcher strips it.

Gazette file exchange uses two directional files in the officium exchange directory. Every jjx MCP call unconditionally deletes both gazette files on entry (read+delete `gazette_in.md`, delete `gazette_out.md`). Gazette content has single-MCP-call lifetime — it is a parameter or a return value, not persistent state.

- **`gazette_in.md`** (agent → server): write before calling a setter command (`jjx_enroll`, `jjx_redocket`, `jjx_paddock` setter).
- **`gazette_out.md`** (server → agent): written by getter commands, read after they return. The next jjx call of any kind deletes it.
  - `jjx_orient` → `# jjezs_paddock <firemark>` + paddock content, `# jjezs_pace <coronet>` + docket content (for next actionable pace)
  - `jjx_show` (with detail) → `# jjezs_paddock <firemark>` + paddock content, `# jjezs_pace <coronet>` + docket per pace
  - `jjx_paddock` (getter) → `# jjezs_paddock <firemark>` + paddock content

**Gazette wire format (setter commands):**
Each notice is a `#`-header line with slug and lede, followed by content body. The lede is **exactly one whitespace-free token** (silks / coronet / firemark) — nothing follows it on the `#` line; the body goes on the lines beneath. This is uniform across every input slug: `jjezs_slate` takes silks, `jjezs_reslate` takes a coronet, `jjezs_paddock` takes a firemark, and in each case appending extra text to the lede folds it into the identity and fails validation. Write `gazette_in.md`, then call the command.

**Critical: `#` (H1) in gazette_in.md is a wire format delimiter, NOT a markdown heading.** For single-notice commands (enroll, paddock set), use exactly ONE `#` line. For mass reslate, each `# jjezs_reslate` line starts a new notice. All markdown headings within body content must use `##` or deeper — a bare `#` line inside content will be parsed as a notice boundary.

Wrong (parsed as TWO notices, fails with `unknown slug 'Paddock:'`):
```
# jjezs_paddock ₣BO
# Paddock: rbk-11-mvp-tactical
body...
```

Right:
```
# jjezs_paddock ₣BO
## Paddock: rbk-11-mvp-tactical
body...
```

**On failure:** If a gazette setter command fails, `gazette_in.md` is already consumed (deleted on entry). You must re-write it from scratch before retrying — there is nothing to re-read.

| Command | Write to `gazette_in.md` | Then call with params |
|---------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| `jjx_enroll` | `# jjezs_slate <silks>` + docket body | `{"firemark": "XX", "before?": ..., "after?": ..., "first?": ...}` |
| `jjx_redocket` | `# jjezs_reslate <coronet>` + docket body | `{}` (coronet is in gazette lede) |
| `jjx_redocket` (mass) | Multiple `# jjezs_reslate <coronet>` notices, each with docket body | `{}` |
| `jjx_paddock` (set) | `# jjezs_paddock <firemark>` + content body | `{"note?": "commit annotation"}` |

Gazette paths: `.claude/jjm/officia/<officium-id>/gazette_in.md` and `gazette_out.md`.

Example — reslate a pace docket:
1. Write `gazette_in.md`: `# jjezs_reslate AvAAH\n\n## Character\nNew docket content...`
2. Call: `jjx_redocket` with `{}` (coronet parsed from gazette lede)

Example — mass reslate multiple paces:
1. Write `gazette_in.md` with multiple notices: `# jjezs_reslate AvAAH\n\nFirst docket...\n# jjezs_reslate AvAAI\n\nSecond docket...`
2. Call: `jjx_redocket` with `{}`

**Read-modify-write workflow** (paddock editing):
1. Call `jjx_paddock` getter → reads `gazette_out.md`
2. Rename `gazette_out.md` → `gazette_in.md`, edit content
3. Call `jjx_paddock` setter

### Mount Protocol

When user says "mount" or you need to engage the next pace:

1. Run `jjx_orient` command with the firemark. **Firemark is required** — if the user says "mount" without specifying a heat and you have no prior heat context in this session, ask which heat to mount rather than guessing. If you have prior context (previously mounted/groomed heat), use that firemark.
2. Parse output: Racing-heats table, Heat/Next/Docket/Recent-work sections. Read paddock and pace docket from the gazette file written to the officium exchange directory.
3. **Read the paddock before the docket.** The paddock tells you the shape of the work and what's been learned; the docket tells you what to do next. Orientation before action.
4. **Lead with the pace goal in one sentence**, distilled from the docket — what this pace is trying to accomplish, stated precisely. Paces can sit weeks between slating and mounting; the operator needs goal recall first, not heat scenery. Then display brief heat context (silks + paddock one-liner + recent work) and finally the full docket.
5. **Name assessment**: If pace silks doesn't fit docket, offer rename via `jjx_relabel`
6. Analyze docket, propose approach (2-4 bullets), assess execution strategy:
   - Model tier: haiku (mechanical), sonnet (standard dev), opus (architectural)
   - Parallelization: file independence, task decomposability
   - State recommendation explicitly (e.g., "Sequential sonnet — single file")
7. Ask to proceed, then begin work

### Groom Protocol

When user says "groom":

1. Run `jjx_show` command with `{target: FIREMARK, detail: true, remaining: true}`
2. Read `gazette_out.md` for full paddock and pace docket content
3. Display overview: heat silks, progress, remaining paces with dockets (from gazette)
4. Enter planning mode: suggest structural operations (slate new paces, rail to reorder, reslate to refine dockets, paddock review)

### Foray Protocol

When user says "foray" or asks to run something on a remote machine:

**Concepts:**
- **Legatio**: A standing SSH session to a remote project (fundus). Created by `jjx_bind`, identified by a token (L0, L1, ...). Persists for the officium lifetime.
- **Pensum**: An async dispatched job within a legatio. Created by `jjx_relay`, identified by a `₱`-prefixed token. Each pensum maps to a remote temp directory on the fundus.
- **Curia**: The invoking side (where Claude runs). **Fundus**: The executing side (where the tabtarget runs).

**Fundus constants** (use these for `jjx_bind`): the default reldir and BURN alias are project-specific — get them from your project's veiled `claude-jjk-*.md` (if present) or the target's BUK Regime Node profile.

**Workflow:**

1. **Bind** (once per session per host): `jjx_bind` with `{alias: "<BURN_ALIAS>", reldir: "<your-project-reldir>"}`. Resolves the BURN profile on the curia to extract host/user/command, then probes via SSH. Returns legatio token.
2. **Ensure curia is clean and pushed**: notch if needed, `git push` if needed. `jjx_relay` will refuse dispatch if working tree is dirty or HEAD is unpushed.
3. **Plant**: `jjx_plant` with `{legatio: "L0", commit: "<HEAD SHA>"}`. Resets fundus to exact commit.
4. **Relay**: `jjx_relay` with `{legatio: "L0", tabtarget: "<filename>.sh", timeout: <seconds>, firemark: "<heat>"}`. Returns pensum token.
5. **Check**: `jjx_check` with `{pensum: "<token>", timeout: 0}` for instant probe, or `timeout: <seconds>` to poll until terminal. Returns BURX fields + liveness report. On terminal status, also returns file list from remote temp dir.
6. **Fetch** (selective): `jjx_fetch` with `{legatio: "L0", path: "<absolute-path>"}` to retrieve specific result files. Use paths from the check file list.

**Liveness states** (from `jjx_check`):
- **running**: BURX_EXIT_STATUS absent, PID alive
- **orphaned**: BURX_EXIT_STATUS absent, PID dead (crashed without writing status)
- **stopped**: BURX_EXIT_STATUS present (check exit code)
- **lost**: burx.env file missing

**`jjx_send`** is for synchronous one-off commands (no pensum, no polling). Use when the command is short and you need inline results.

**Windows fundus body discipline:** When the fundus is a Windows host, both `jjx_send` and `jjx_relay`-dispatched tabtargets traverse the cmd.exe → wsl.exe / cygwin / PowerShell transport stack. Body authoring rules (escape `\$name` for w-letter wsl.exe transit, no heredocs, single-line `;`-joined for cmd.exe transit, lazy-flush avoidance for PowerShell cmdlets, etc.) live in your project's Windows scripting guide, if present. Empirical record under `Memos/memo-YYYYMMDD-windows-transport-{topic}.md`.

### Commit Discipline

When working on a heat, use `jjx_record` for commits with heat/pace affiliation.

**Pace-affiliated commit** (active pace provides context):
Use `jjx_record` with `{identity: "CORONET", files: ["file1", "file2"], intent: "description"}`

**Heat-affiliated commit** (no active pace, but part of heat work):
Use `jjx_record` with `{identity: "FIREMARK", files: ["file1", "file2"], intent: "description"}`

Synthesize intent from the conversation — describe *what* was accomplished, not *how*.

**Size guard — ALL commands accepting `size_limit?` (record, close, archive, paddock, enroll, redocket, transfer)**: If ANY jjx command fails due to size limits, STOP. Report the byte count, limit, and per-file breakdown to the user, then WAIT for the user's review. The guard is a byte-sanity review step — its purpose is catching unintended bulk (e.g., a binary that snuck in), not gating decomposition. The default is uniform 50KB across all seven commands. Expected outcome: the user confirms the bytes are legitimate and directs you to raise the limit. Do NOT offer splitting as a parallel option — splitting enters the picture only if the user explicitly raises it, because the work genuinely decomposes. NEVER auto-override `size_limit`.

When user says "notch", determine context (pace or heat affiliated) and invoke `jjx_record` with the appropriate identity and explicit file list.

**Multi-Officium Discipline:**
Multiple Claude officia (concurrent chat sessions, each with its own ☉-prefixed officium ID) may work concurrently in the same repo. The explicit file list in `jjx_record` enables orthogonal commits.

- Claude is **additive only** — make commits, never discard changes

   275	
   276	**Multi-Officium Discipline:**
   277	Multiple Claude officia (concurrent chat sessions, each with its own ☉-prefixed officium ID) may work concurrently in the same repo. The explicit file list in `jjx_record` enables orthogonal commits.
   278	
   279	- Claude is **additive only** — make commits, never discard changes
   280	- "Unexpected" uncommitted changes are likely another officium's work
   281	- If something looks wrong, ASK — do not "fix" by discarding
   282	- Commit only YOUR files; ignore everything else
   283	
   284	**Forbidden Git Commands — NO exceptions, NO "safe" variants:**
   285	- `git reset` — ALL forms: `--hard`, `--soft`, `--mixed`, with paths, without paths. Even `git reset HEAD <file>` (unstaging) is forbidden — it's too close to destructive variants and Claude will reason its way into worse forms.
   286	- `git restore` — ALL forms: working tree, staged, with `--source`, without
   287	- `git checkout <file>` — when used to discard changes (navigating branches is fine)
   288	- `git clean` — ALL forms
   289	- `git stash` — ALL forms
   290	
   291	**What to do instead:**
   292	- Staging wrong? Run `jjx_record` with the correct file list — it handles staging
   293	- Made a mistake? Make a new commit that fixes it — additive, not destructive
   294	- Confused by repo state? ASK the user — another officium may be mid-work
   295	- Need to undo something? Explain the situation to the user and let them decide
   296	
   297	**JJX Commands Are Self-Committing:**
   298	`jjx_enroll`, `jjx_close`, `jjx_record`, and other state-mutating jjx commands create git commits internally. **`jjx_close` (wrap) commits ALL uncommitted changes** — code files and gallops state together in one commit.
