150	2. **On every subsequent jjx call**, pass the returned identity as the `officium` field on the MCP tool (sibling to `command` and `params`).
   151	3. **Self-healing**: If any jjx command fails with "Officium directory not found", call `jjx_open` again to create a fresh officium, then retry.
   152	
   153	The ☉ (U+2609 SUN) prefix parallels ₣/₢ for firemarks/coronets. Pass it exactly as returned — the dispatcher strips it.
   154	
   155	Gazette file exchange uses two directional files in the officium exchange directory. Every jjx MCP call unconditionally deletes both gazette files on entry (read+delete `gazette_in.md`, delete `gazette_out.md`). Gazette content has single-MCP-call lifetime — it is a parameter or a return value, not persistent state.
   156	
   157	- **`gazette_in.md`** (agent → server): write before calling a setter command (`jjx_enroll`, `jjx_redocket`, `jjx_paddock` setter).
   158	- **`gazette_out.md`** (server → agent): written by getter commands, read after they return. The next jjx call of any kind deletes it.
   159	  - `jjx_orient` → `# jjezs_paddock <firemark>` + paddock content, `# jjezs_pace <coronet>` + docket content (for next actionable pace)
   160	  - `jjx_show` (with detail) → `# jjezs_paddock <firemark>` + paddock content, `# jjezs_pace <coronet>` + docket per pace
   161	  - `jjx_paddock` (getter) → `# jjezs_paddock <firemark>` + paddock content
   162	
   163	**Gazette wire format (setter commands):**
   164	Each notice is a `#`-header line with slug and lede, followed by content body. The lede is **exactly one whitespace-free token** (silks / coronet / firemark) — nothing follows it on the `#` line; the body goes on the lines beneath. This is uniform across every input slug: `jjezs_slate` takes silks, `jjezs_reslate` takes a coronet, `jjezs_paddock` takes a firemark, and in each case appending extra text to the lede folds it into the identity and fails validation. Write `gazette_in.md`, then call the command.
   165	
   166	**Critical: `#` (H1) in gazette_in.md is a wire format delimiter, NOT a markdown heading.** For single-notice commands (enroll, paddock set), use exactly ONE `#` line. For mass reslate, each `# jjezs_reslate` line starts a new notice. All markdown headings within body content must use `##` or deeper — a bare `#` line inside content will be parsed as a notice boundary.
   167	
   168	Wrong (parsed as TWO notices, fails with `unknown slug 'Paddock:'`):
   169	```
   170	# jjezs_paddock ₣BO
   171	# Paddock: rbk-11-mvp-tactical
   172	body...
   173	```
   174	
   175	Right:
   176	```
   177	# jjezs_paddock ₣BO
   178	## Paddock: rbk-11-mvp-tactical
   179	body...
   180	```
   181	
   182	**On failure:** If a gazette setter command fails, `gazette_in.md` is already consumed (deleted on entry). You must re-write it from scratch before retrying — there is nothing to re-read.
   183	
   184	| Command | Write to `gazette_in.md` | Then call with params |
   185	|---------|--------------------------|----------------------|
   186	| `jjx_enroll` | `# jjezs_slate <silks>` + docket body | `{"firemark": "XX", "before?": ..., "after?": ..., "first?": ...}` |
   187	| `jjx_redocket` | `# jjezs_reslate <coronet>` + docket body | `{}` (coronet is in gazette lede) |
   188	| `jjx_redocket` (mass) | Multiple `# jjezs_reslate <coronet>` notices, each with docket body | `{}` |
   189	| `jjx_paddock` (set) | `# jjezs_paddock <firemark>` + content body | `{"note?": "commit annotation"}` |
   190	
   191	Gazette paths: `.claude/jjm/officia/<officium-id>/gazette_in.md` and `gazette_out.md`.
   192	
   193	Example — reslate a pace docket:
   194	1. Write `gazette_in.md`: `# jjezs_reslate AvAAH\n\n## Character\nNew docket content...`
   195	2. Call: `jjx_redocket` with `{}` (coronet parsed from gazette lede)
   196	
   197	Example — mass reslate multiple paces:
   198	1. Write `gazette_in.md` with multiple notices: `# jjezs_reslate AvAAH\n\nFirst docket...\n# jjezs_reslate AvAAI\n\nSecond docket...`
   199	2. Call: `jjx_redocket` with `{}`
   200	
   201	**Read-modify-write workflow** (paddock editing):
   202	1. Call `jjx_paddock` getter → reads `gazette_out.md`
   203	2. Rename `gazette_out.md` → `gazette_in.md`, edit content
   204	3. Call `jjx_paddock` setter
   205	
   206	### Mount Protocol
   207	
   208	When user says "mount" or you need to engage the next pace:
   209	
   210	1. Run `jjx_orient` command with the firemark. **Firemark is required** — if the user says "mount" without specifying a heat and you have no prior heat context in this session, ask which heat to mount rather than guessing. If you have prior context (previously mounted/groomed heat), use that firemark.
   211	2. Parse output: Racing-heats table, Heat/Next/Docket/Recent-work sections. Read paddock and pace docket from the gazette file written to the officium exchange directory.
   212	3. **Read the paddock before the docket.** The paddock tells you the shape of the work and what's been learned; the docket tells you what to do next. Orientation before action.
   213	4. **Lead with the pace goal in one sentence**, distilled from the docket — what this pace is trying to accomplish, stated precisely. Paces can sit weeks between slating and mounting; the operator needs goal recall first, not heat scenery. Then display brief heat context (silks + paddock one-liner + recent work) and finally the full docket.
   214	5. **Name assessment**: If pace silks doesn't fit docket, offer rename via `jjx_relabel`
   215	6. Analyze docket, propose approach (2-4 bullets), assess execution strategy:
   216	   - Model tier: haiku (mechanical), sonnet (standard dev), opus (architectural)
   217	   - Parallelization: file independence, task decomposability
   218	   - State recommendation explicitly (e.g., "Sequential sonnet — single file")
   219	7. Ask to proceed, then begin work
   220	
   221	### Groom Protocol
   222	
   223	When user says "groom":
   224	
   225	1. Run `jjx_show` command with `{target: FIREMARK, detail: true, remaining: true}`
   226	2. Read `gazette_out.md` for full paddock and pace docket content
   227	3. Display overview: heat silks, progress, remaining paces with dockets (from gazette)
   228	4. Enter planning mode: suggest structural operations (slate new paces, rail to reorder, reslate to refine dockets, paddock review)
   229	
   230	### Foray Protocol
   231	
   232	When user says "foray" or asks to run something on a remote machine:
   233	
   234	**Concepts:**
   235	- **Legatio**: A standing SSH session to a remote project (fundus). Created by `jjx_bind`, identified by a token (L0, L1, ...). Persists for the officium lifetime.
   236	- **Pensum**: An async dispatched job within a legatio. Created by `jjx_relay`, identified by a `₱`-prefixed token. Each pensum maps to a remote temp directory on the fundus.
   237	- **Curia**: The invoking side (where Claude runs). **Fundus**: The executing side (where the tabtarget runs).
   238	
   239	**Fundus constants** (use these for `jjx_bind`): the default reldir and BURN alias are project-specific — get them from your project's veiled `claude-jjk-*.md` (if present) or the target's BUK Regime Node profile.
   240	
   241	**Workflow:**
   242	
   243	1. **Bind** (once per session per host): `jjx_bind` with `{alias: "<BURN_ALIAS>", reldir: "<your-project-reldir>"}`. Resolves the BURN profile on the curia to extract host/user/command, then probes via SSH. Returns legatio token.
   244	2. **Ensure curia is clean and pushed**: notch if needed, `git push` if needed. `jjx_relay` will refuse dispatch if working tree is dirty or HEAD is unpushed.
   245	3. **Plant**: `jjx_plant` with `{legatio: "L0", commit: "<HEAD SHA>"}`. Resets fundus to exact commit.
   246	4. **Relay**: `jjx_relay` with `{legatio: "L0", tabtarget: "<filename>.sh", timeout: <seconds>, firemark: "<heat>"}`. Returns pensum token.
   247	5. **Check**: `jjx_check` with `{pensum: "<token>", timeout: 0}` for instant probe, or `timeout: <seconds>` to poll until terminal. Returns BURX fields + liveness report. On terminal status, also returns file list from remote temp dir.
   248	6. **Fetch** (selective): `jjx_fetch` with `{legatio: "L0", path: "<absolute-path>"}` to retrieve specific result files. Use paths from the check file list.
   249	
   250	**Liveness states** (from `jjx_check`):
   251	- **running**: BURX_EXIT_STATUS absent, PID alive
   252	- **orphaned**: BURX_EXIT_STATUS absent, PID dead (crashed without writing status)
   253	- **stopped**: BURX_EXIT_STATUS present (check exit code)
   254	- **lost**: burx.env file missing
   255	
   256	**`jjx_send`** is for synchronous one-off commands (no pensum, no polling). Use when the command is short and you need inline results.
   257	
   258	**Windows fundus body discipline:** When the fundus is a Windows host, both `jjx_send` and `jjx_relay`-dispatched tabtargets traverse the cmd.exe → wsl.exe / cygwin / PowerShell transport stack. Body authoring rules (escape `\$name` for w-letter wsl.exe transit, no heredocs, single-line `;`-joined for cmd.exe transit, lazy-flush avoidance for PowerShell cmdlets, etc.) live in your project's Windows scripting guide, if present. Empirical record under `Memos/memo-YYYYMMDD-windows-transport-{topic}.md`.
   259	
   260	### Commit Discipline
   261	
   262	When working on a heat, use `jjx_record` for commits with heat/pace affiliation.
   263	
   264	**Pace-affiliated commit** (active pace provides context):
   265	Use `jjx_record` with `{identity: "CORONET", files: ["file1", "file2"], intent: "description"}`
   266	
   267	**Heat-affiliated commit** (no active pace, but part of heat work):
   268	Use `jjx_record` with `{identity: "FIREMARK", files: ["file1", "file2"], intent: "description"}`
   269	
   270	Synthesize intent from the conversation — describe *what* was accomplished, not *how*.
   271	
   272	**Size guard — ALL commands accepting `size_limit?` (record, close, archive, paddock, enroll, redocket, transfer)**: If ANY jjx command fails due to size limits, STOP. Report the byte count, limit, and per-file breakdown to the user, then WAIT for the user's review. The guard is a byte-sanity review step — its purpose is catching unintended bulk (e.g., a binary that snuck in), not gating decomposition. The default is uniform 50KB across all seven commands. Expected outcome: the user confirms the bytes are legitimate and directs you to raise the limit. Do NOT offer splitting as a parallel option — splitting enters the picture only if the user explicitly raises it, because the work genuinely decomposes. NEVER auto-override `size_limit`.
   273	
   274	When user says "notch", determine context (pace or heat affiliated) and invoke `jjx_record` with the appropriate identity and explicit file list.
   275	
   276	**Multi-Officium Discipline:**
   277	Multiple Claude officia (concurrent chat sessions, each with its own ☉-prefixed officium ID) may work concurrently in the same repo. The explicit file list in `jjx_record` enables orthogonal commits.
   278	
   279	- Claude is **additive only** — make commits, never discard changes
   280	- "Unexpected" uncommitted changes are likely another officium's work
   281	- If something looks wrong, ASK — do not "fix" by discarding
   282	- Commit only YOUR files; ignore everything else
   283	
   284	**Forbidden Git Commands — NO exceptions, NO "safe" variants:**
   285	- `git reset` — ALL forms: `--hard`, `--soft`, `--mixed`, with paths, without paths. Even `git reset HEAD <file>` (unstaging) is forbidden — it's too close to destructive variants and Claude will reason its way into worse forms.
   286	- `git restore` — ALL forms: working tree, staged, with `--source`, without
   287	- `git checkout <file>` — when used to discard changes (navigating branches is fine)
   288	- `git clean` — ALL forms
   289	- `git stash` — ALL forms
   290	
   291	**What to do instead:**
   292	- Staging wrong? Run `jjx_record` with the correct file list — it handles staging
   293	- Made a mistake? Make a new commit that fixes it — additive, not destructive
   294	- Confused by repo state? ASK the user — another officium may be mid-work
   295	- Need to undo something? Explain the situation to the user and let them decide
   296	
   297	**JJX Commands Are Self-Committing:**
   298	`jjx_enroll`, `jjx_close`, `jjx_record`, and other state-mutating jjx commands create git commits internally. **`jjx_close` (wrap) commits ALL uncommitted changes** — code files and gallops state together in one commit. Do NOT follow `jjx_record` or `jjx_close` with another commit command — the tree will already be clean. If a commit command says "Nothing to commit", check `git status --short` and accept the result.
   299	
   300	**Diagnose Before Escalating:**

body...
```

**On failure:** If a gazette setter command fails, `gazette_in.md` is already consumed (deleted on entry). You must re-write it from scratch before retrying — there is nothing to re-read.

| Command | Write to `gazette_in.md` | Then call with params |
|---------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| `jjx_enroll` | `# jjezs_slate <silks>` + docket body | `{"firemark": "XX", "before?": ..., "after?": ..., "first?": ...}` |
| `jjx_redocket` | `# jjezs_reslate <coronet>` + docket body | `{}` (coronet is in gazette lede) |
| `jjx_redocket` (mass) | Multiple `# jjezs_reslate <coronet>` notices, each with docket body | `{}` |
| `jjx_paddock` (set) | `# jjezs_paddock <firemark>` + content body | `{"note?": "commit annotation"}` |

Gazette paths: `.claude/jjm/officia/<officium-id>/gazette_in.md` and `gazette_out.md`.

Example — reslate a pace docket:
1. Write `gazette_in.md`: `# jjezs_reslate AvAAH\n\n## Character\nNew docket content...`
2. Call: `jjx_redocket` with `{}` (coronet parsed from gazette lede)

Example — mass reslate multiple paces:
1. Write `gazette_in.md` with multiple notices: `# jjezs_reslate AvAAH\n\nFirst docket...\n# jjezs_reslate AvAAI\n\nSecond docket...`
2. Call: `jjx_redocket` with `{}`

**Read-modify-write workflow** (paddock editing):
1. Call `jjx_paddock` getter → reads `gazette_out.md`
2. Rename `gazette_out.md` → `gazette_in.md`, edit content
3. Call `jjx_paddock` setter

### Mount Protocol

When user says "mount" or you need to engage the next pace:

1. Run `jjx_orient` command with the firemark. **Firemark is required** — if the user says "mount" without specifying a heat and you have no prior heat context in this session, ask which heat to mount rather than guessing. If you have prior context (previously mounted/groomed heat), use that firemark.
2. Parse output: Racing-heats table, Heat/Next/Docket/Recent-work sections. Read paddock and pace docket from the gazette file written to the officium exchange directory.
3. **Read the paddock before the docket.** The paddock tells you the shape of the work and what's been learned; the docket tells you what to do next. Orientation before action.
4. **Lead with the pace goal in one sentence**, distilled from the docket — what this pace is trying to accomplish, stated precisely. Paces can sit weeks between slating and mounting; the operator needs goal recall first, not heat scenery. Then display brief heat context (silks + paddock one-liner + recent work) and finally the full docket.
5. **Name assessment**: If pace silks doesn't fit docket, offer rename via `jjx_relabel`
6. Analyze docket, propose approach (2-4 bullets), assess execution strategy:
   - Model tier: haiku (mechanical), sonnet (standard dev), opus (architectural)
   - Parallelization: file independence, task decomposability
   - State recommendation explicitly (e.g., "Sequential sonnet — single file")
7. Ask to proceed, then begin work

### Groom Protocol

When user says "groom":

1. Run `jjx_show` command with `{target: FIREMARK, detail: true, remaining: true}`
2. Read `gazette_out.md` for full paddock and pace docket content
3. Display overview: heat silks, progress, remaining paces with dockets (from gazette)
4. Enter planning mode: suggest structural operations (slate new paces, rail to reorder, reslate to refine dockets, paddock review)

### Foray Protocol

When user says "foray" or asks to run something on a remote machine:

**Concepts:**
- **Legatio**: A standing SSH session to a remote project (fundus). Created by `jjx_bind`, identified by a token (L0, L1, ...). Persists for the officium lifetime.
- **Pensum**: An async dispatched job within a legatio. Created by `jjx_relay`, identified by a `₱`-prefixed token. Each pensum maps to a remote temp directory on the fundus.
- **Curia**: The invoking side (where Claude runs). **Fundus**: The executing side (where the tabtarget runs).

**Fundus constants** (use these for `jjx_bind`): the default reldir and BURN alias are project-specific — get them from your project's veiled `claude-jjk-*.md` (if present) or the target's BUK Regime Node profile.

**Workflow:**

1. **Bind** (once per session per host): `jjx_bind` with `{alias: "<BURN_ALIAS>", reldir: "<your-project-reldir>"}`. Resolves the BURN profile on the curia to extract host/user/command, then probes via SSH. Returns legatio token.
2. **Ensure curia is clean and pushed**: notch if needed, `git push` if needed. `jjx_relay` will refuse dispatch if working tree is dirty or HEAD is unpushed.
3. **Plant**: `jjx_plant` with `{legatio: "L0", commit: "<HEAD SHA>"}`. Resets fundus to exact commit.
4. **Relay**: `jjx_relay` with `{legatio: "L0", tabtarget: "<filename>.sh", timeout: <seconds>, firemark: "<heat>"}`. Returns pensum token.
5. **Check**: `jjx_check` with `{pensum: "<token>", timeout: 0}` for instant probe, or `timeout: <seconds>` to poll until terminal. Returns BURX fields + liveness report. On terminal status, also returns file list from remote temp dir.
6. **Fetch** (selective): `jjx_fetch` with `{legatio: "L0", path: "<absolute-path>"}` to retrieve specific result files. Use paths from the check file list.

**Liveness states** (from `jjx_check`):
- **running**: BURX_EXIT_STATUS absent, PID alive
- **orphaned**: BURX_EXIT_STATUS absent, PID dead (crashed without writing status)
- **stopped**: BURX_EXIT_STATUS present (check exit code)
- **lost**: burx.env file missing

**`jjx_send`** is for synchronous one-off commands (no pensum, no polling). Use when the command is short and you need inline results.

**Windows fundus body discipline:** When the fundus is a Windows host, both `jjx_send` and `jjx_relay`-dispatched tabtargets traverse the cmd.exe → wsl.exe / cygwin / PowerShell transport stack. Body authoring rules (escape `\$name` for w-letter wsl.exe transit, no heredocs, single-line `;`-joined for cmd.exe transit, lazy-flush avoidance for PowerShell cmdlets, etc.) live in your project's Windows scripting guide, if present. Empirical record under `Memos/memo-YYYYMMDD-windows-transport-{topic}.md`.

### Commit Discipline

When working on a heat, use `jjx_record` for commits with heat/pace affiliation.

**Pace-affiliated commit** (active pace provides context):
Use `jjx_record` with `{identity: "CORONET", files: ["file1", "file2"], intent: "description"}`

**Heat-affiliated commit** (no active pace, but part of heat work):
Use `jjx_record` with `{identity: "FIREMARK", files: ["file1", "file2"], intent: "description"}`

Body authoring rules (escape `\$name` for w-letter wsl.exe transit, no heredocs, single-line `;`-joined for cmd.exe transit, lazy-flush avoidance for PowerShell cmdlets, etc.) live in your project's Windows scripting guide, if present. Empirical record under `Memos/memo-YYYYMMDD-windows-transport-{topic}.md`.
259	
260	### Commit Discipline
261	
262	When working on a heat, use `jjx_record` for commits with heat/pace affiliation.
263	
264	**Pace-affiliated commit** (active pace provides context):
265	Use `jjx_record` with `{identity: "CORONET", files: ["file1", "file2"], intent: "description"}`
266	
267	**Heat-affiliated commit** (no active pace, but part of heat work):
268	Use `jjx_record` with `{identity: "FIREMARK", files: ["file1", "file2"], intent: "description"}`
269	
270	Synthesize intent from the conversation — describe *what* was accomplished, not *how*.
271	
272	**Size guard — ALL commands accepting `size_limit?` (record, close, archive, paddock, enroll, redocket, transfer)**: If ANY jjx command fails due to size limits, STOP. Report the byte count, limit, and per-file breakdown to the user, then WAIT for the user's review. The guard is a byte-sanity review step — its purpose is catching unintended bulk (e.g., a binary that snuck in), not gating decomposition. The default is uniform 50KB across all seven commands. Expected outcome: the user confirms the bytes are legitimate and directs you to raise the limit. Do NOT offer splitting as a parallel option — splitting enters the picture only if the user explicitly raises it, because the work genuinely decomposes. NEVER auto-override `size_limit`.
273	
274	When user says "notch", determine context (pace or heat affiliated) and invoke `jjx_record` with the appropriate identity and explicit file list.

162	
163	**Gazette wire format (setter commands):**
164	Each notice is a `#`-header line with slug and lede, followed by content body. The lede is **exactly one whitespace-free token** (silks / coronet / firemark) — nothing follows it on the `#` line; the body goes on the lines beneath. This is uniform across every input slug: `jjezs_slate` takes silks, `jjezs_reslate` takes a coronet, `jjezs_paddock` takes a firemark, and in each case appending extra text to the lede folds it into the identity and fails validation. Write `gazette_in.md`, then call the command.
165

For mass reslate, each `# jjezs_reslate` line starts a new notice. - `jjx_show` (with detail) → `# jjezs_paddock <firemark>` + paddock content, `# jjezs_pace <coronet>` + docket per pace
  - `jjx_paddock` (getter) → `# jjezs_paddock <firemark>` + paddock content

**Gazette wire format (setter commands):**
Each notice is a `#`-header line with slug and lede, followed by content body. The lede is **exactly one whitespace-free token** (silks / coronet / firemark) — nothing follows it on the `#` line; the body goes on the lines beneath. This is uniform across every input slug: `jjezs_slate` takes silks, `jjezs_reslate` takes a coronet, `jjezs_paddock` takes a firemark, and in each case appending extra text to the lede folds it into the identity and fails validation. Write `gazette_in.md`, then call the command.

**Critical: `#` (H1) in gazette_in.md is a wire format delimiter, NOT a markdown heading.** For single-notice commands (enroll, paddock set), use exactly ONE `#` line. For mass reslate, each `# jjezs_reslate` line starts a new notice. All markdown headings within body content must use `##` or deeper — a bare `#` line inside content will be parsed as a notice boundary.

Wrong (parsed as TWO notices, fails with `unknown slug 'Paddock:'`):
```
# jjezs_paddock ₣BO
# Paddock: rbk-11-mvp-tactical
body...
```

Right:
```
# jjezs_paddock ₣BO
## Paddock: rbk-11-mvp-tactical
body...
```

**On failure:** If a gazette setter command fails, `gazette_in.md` is already consumed (deleted on entry). You must re-write it from scratch before retrying — there is nothing to re-read.

| Command | Write to `gazette_in.md` | Then call with params |
|---------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| `jjx_enroll` | `# jjezs_slate <silks>` + docket body | `{"firemark": "XX", "before?": ..., "after?": ..., "first?": ...}` |
| `jjx_redocket` | `# jjezs_reslate <coronet>` + docket body | `{}` (coronet is in gazette lede) |
| `jjx_redocket` (mass) | Multiple `# jjezs_reslate <coronet>` notices, each with docket body | `{}` |
| `jjx_paddock` (set) | `# jjezs_paddock <firemark>` + content body | `{"note?": "commit annotation"}` |

Gazette paths: `.claude/jjm/officia/<officium-id>/gazette_in.md` and `gazette_out.md`.

Example — reslate a pace docket:
1. Write `gazette_in.md`: `# jjezs_reslate AvAAH\n\n## Character\nNew docket content...`
2. Call: `jjx_redocket` with `{}` (coronet parsed from gazette lede)

Example — mass reslate multiple paces:
1. Write `gazette_in.md` with multiple notices: `# jjezs_reslate AvAAH\n\nFirst docket...\n# jjezs_reslate AvAAI\n\nSecond docket...`
2. Call: `jjx_redocket` with `{}`

**Read-modify-write workflow** (paddock editing):
1. Call `jjx_paddock` getter → reads `gazette_out.md`
2. Rename `gazette_out.md` → `gazette_in.md`, edit content
3. Call `jjx_paddock` setter

### Mount Protocol

When user says "mount" or you need to engage the next pace:

1. Run `jjx_orient` command with the firemark. **Firemark is required** — if the user says "mount" without specifying a heat and you have no prior heat context in this session, ask which heat to mount rather than guessing. If you have prior context (previously mounted/groomed heat), use that firemark.
2. Parse output: Racing-heats table, Heat/Next/Docket/Recent-work sections. Read paddock and pace docket from the gazette file written to the officium exchange directory.
3. **Read the paddock before the docket.** The paddock tells you the shape of the work and what's been learned; the docket tells you what to do next. Orientation before action.
4. **Lead with the pace goal in one sentence**, distilled from the docket — what this pace is trying to accomplish, stated precisely. Paces can sit weeks between slating and mounting; the operator needs goal recall first, not heat scenery. Then display brief heat context (silks + paddock one-liner + recent work) and finally the full docket.
5. **Name assessment**: If pace silks doesn't fit docket, offer rename via `jjx_relabel`
6. Analyze docket, propose approach (2-4 bullets), assess execution strategy:
   - Model tier: haiku (mechanical), sonnet (standard dev), opus (architectural)
   - Parallelization: file independence, task decomposability
   - State recommendation explicitly (e.g., "Sequential sonnet — single file")
7. Ask to proceed, then begin work

Gazette file exchange uses two directional files in the officium exchange directory. Every jjx MCP call unconditionally deletes both gazette files on entry (read+delete `gazette_in.md`, delete `gazette_out.md`). Gazette content has single-MCP-call lifetime — it is a parameter or a return value, not persistent state.

- **`gazette_in.md`** (agent → server): write before calling a setter command (`jjx_enroll`, `jjx_redocket`, `jjx_paddock` setter).
- **`gazette_out.md`** (server → agent): written by getter commands, read after they return. The next jjx call of any kind deletes it.
  - `jjx_orient` → `# jjezs_paddock <firemark>` + paddock content, `# jjezs_pace <coronet>` + docket content (for next actionable pace)
  - `jjx_show` (with detail) → `# jjezs_paddock <firemark>` + paddock content, `# jjezs_pace <coronet>` + docket per pace
  - `jjx_paddock` (getter) → `# jjezs_paddock <firemark>` + paddock content

**Gazette wire format (setter commands):**
Each notice is a `#`-header line with slug and lede, followed by content body. The lede is **exactly one whitespace-free token** (silks / coronet / firemark) — nothing follows it on the `#` line; the body goes on the lines beneath. This is uniform across every input slug: `jjezs_slate` takes silks, `jjezs_reslate` takes a coronet, `jjezs_paddock` takes a firemark, and in each case appending extra text to the lede folds it into the identity and fails validation. Write `gazette_in.md`, then call the command.

**Critical: `#` (H1) in gazette_in.md is a wire format delimiter, NOT a markdown heading.** For single-notice commands (enroll, paddock set), use exactly ONE `#` line. For mass reslate, each `# jjezs_reslate` line starts a new notice. All markdown headings within body content must use `##` or deeper — a bare `#` line inside content will be parsed as a notice boundary.

Wrong (parsed as TWO notices, fails with `unknown slug 'Paddock:'`):
```
# jjezs_paddock ₣BO
# Paddock: rbk-11-mvp-tactical
body...
```

Right:
```
# jjezs_paddock ₣BO
## Paddock: rbk-11-mvp-tactical
body...
```

**On failure:** If a gazette setter command fails, `gazette_in.md` is already consumed (deleted on entry). You must re-write it from scratch before retrying — there is nothing to re-read.

| Command | Write to `gazette_in.md` | Then call with params |
|---------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| `jjx_enroll` | `# jjezs_slate <silks>` + docket body | `{"firemark": "XX", "before?": ..., "after?": ..., "first?": ...}` |
| `jjx_redocket` | `# jjezs_reslate <coronet>` + docket body | `{}` (coronet is in gazette lede) |
| `jjx_redocket` (mass) | Multiple `# jjezs_reslate <coronet>` notices, each with docket body | `{}` |
| `jjx_paddock` (set) | `# jjezs_paddock <firemark>` + content body | `{"note?": "commit annotation"}` |

Gazette paths: `.claude/jjm/officia/<officium-id>/gazette_in.md` and `gazette_out.md`.

Example — reslate a pace docket:
1. Write `gazette_in.md`: `# jjezs_reslate AvAAH\n\n## Character\nNew docket content...`
2. Call: `jjx_redocket` with `{}` (coronet parsed from gazette lede)

Example — mass reslate multiple paces:
1. Write `gazette_in.md` with multiple notices: `# jjezs_reslate AvAAH\n\nFirst docket...\n# jjezs_reslate AvAAI\n\nSecond docket...`
2. Call: `jjx_redocket` with `{}`

**Read-modify-write workflow** (paddock editing):
1. Call `jjx_paddock` getter → reads `gazette_out.md`
2. Rename `gazette_out.md` → `gazette_in.md`, edit content
3. Call `jjx_paddock` setter

### Mount Protocol

When user says "mount" or you need to engage the next pace:

1. Run `jjx_orient` command with the firemark. **Firemark is required** — if the user says "mount" without specifying a heat and you have no prior heat context in this session, ask which heat to mount rather than guessing. If you have prior context (previously mounted/groomed heat), use that firemark.
2. Parse output: Racing-heats table, Heat/Next/Docket/Recent-work sections. Read paddock and pace docket from the gazette file written to the officium exchange directory.
3. **Read the paddock before the docket.** The paddock tells you the shape of the work and what's been learned; the docket tells you what to do next. Orientation before action.
4. **Lead with the pace goal in one sentence**, distilled from the docket — what this pace is trying to accomplish, stated precisely. Paces can sit weeks between slating and mounting; the operator needs goal recall first, not heat scenery. Then display brief heat context (silks + paddock one-liner + recent work) and finally the full docket.
5. **Name assessment**: If pace silks doesn't fit docket, offer rename via `jjx_relabel`
6. Analyze docket, propose approach (2-4 bullets), assess execution strategy:
   - Model tier: haiku (mechanical), sonnet (standard dev), opus (architectural)
   - Parallelization: file independence, task decomposability
   - State recommendation explicitly (e.g., "Sequential sonnet — single file")
7. Ask to proceed, then begin work

### Groom Protocol

When user says "groom":

1. Run `jjx_show` command with `{target: FIREMARK, detail: true, remaining: true}`
2. Read `gazette_out.md` for full paddock and pace docket content
3. Display overview: heat silks, progress, remaining paces with dockets (from gazette)
4. Enter planning mode: suggest structural operations (slate new paces, rail to reorder, reslate to refine dockets, paddock review)

### Foray Protocol

When user says "foray" or asks to run something on a remote machine:

**Concepts:**
- **Legatio**: A standing SSH session to a remote project (fundus). Created by `jjx_bind`, identified by a token (L0, L1, ...). Persists for the officium lifetime.
- **Pensum**: An async dispatched job within a legatio. Created by `jjx_relay`, identified by a `₱`-prefixed token. Each pensum maps to a remote temp directory on the fundus.
- **Curia**: The invoking side (where Claude runs). **Fundus**: The executing side (where the tabtarget runs).

**Fundus constants** (use these for `jjx_bind`): the default reldir and BURN alias are project-specific — get them from your project's veiled `claude-jjk-*.md` (if present) or the target's BUK Regime Node profile.

**Workflow:**

1. **Bind** (once per session per host): `jjx_bind` with `{alias: "<BURN_ALIAS>", reldir: "<your-project-reldir>"}`. Resolves the BURN profile on the curia to extract host/user/command, then probes via SSH. Returns legatio token.
2. **Ensure curia is clean and pushed**: notch if needed, `git push` if needed. `jjx_relay` will refuse dispatch if working tree is dirty or HEAD is unpushed.
3. **Plant**: `jjx_plant` with `{legatio: "L0", commit: "<HEAD SHA>"}`. Resets fundus to exact commit.
4. **Relay**: `jjx_relay` with `{legatio: "L0", tabtarget: "<filename>.sh", timeout: <seconds>, firemark: "<heat>"}`. Returns pensum token.
5. **Check**: `jjx_check` with `{pensum: "<token>", timeout: 0}` for instant probe, or `timeout: <seconds>` to poll until terminal. Returns BURX fields + liveness report. On terminal status, also returns file list from remote temp dir.
6. **Fetch** (selective): `jjx_fetch` with `{legatio: "L0", path: "<absolute-path>"}` to retrieve specific result files. Use paths from the check file list.

**Liveness states** (from `jjx_check`):
- **running**: BURX_EXIT_STATUS absent, PID alive
- **orphaned**: BURX_EXIT_STATUS absent, PID dead (crashed without writing status)
- **stopped**: BURX_EXIT_STATUS present (check exit code)
- **lost**: burx.env file missing

**`jjx_send`** is for synchronous one-off commands (no pensum, no polling). Use when the command is short and you need inline results.

**Windows fundus body discipline:** When the fundus is a Windows host, both `jjx_send` and `jjx_relay`-dispatched tabtargets traverse the cmd.exe → wsl.exe / cygwin / PowerShell transport stack. Body authoring rules (escape `\$name` for w-letter wsl.exe transit, no heredocs, single-line `;`-joined for cmd.exe transit, lazy-flush avoidance for PowerShell cmdlets, etc.) live in your project's Windows scripting guide, if present. Empirical record under `Memos/memo-YYYYMMDD-windows-transport-{topic}.md`.

### Commit Discipline

When working on a heat, use `jjx_record` for commits with heat/pace affiliation.

**Pace-affiliated commit** (active pace provides context):
Use `jjx_record` with `{identity: "CORONET", files: ["file1", "file2"], intent: "description"}`

**Heat-affiliated commit** (no active pace, but part of heat work):
Use `jjx_record` with `{identity: "FIREMARK", files: ["file1", "file2"], intent: "description"}`

Synthesize intent from the conversation — describe *what* was accomplished, not *how*.

**Size guard — ALL commands accepting `size_limit?` (record, close, archive, paddock, enroll, redocket, transfer)**: If ANY jjx command fails due to size limits, STOP. Report the byte count, limit, and per-file breakdown to the user, then WAIT for the user's review. The guard is a byte-sanity review step — its purpose is catching unintended bulk (e.g., a binary that snuck in), not gating decomposition. The default is uniform 50KB across all seven commands. Expected outcome: the user confirms the bytes are legitimate and directs you to raise the limit. Do NOT offer splitting as a parallel option — splitting enters the picture only if the user explicitly raises it, because the work genuinely decomposes. NEVER auto-override `size_limit`.

When user says "notch", determine context (pace or heat affiliated) and invoke `jjx_record` with the appropriate identity and explicit file list.

   - Model tier: haiku (mechanical), sonnet (standard dev), opus (architectural)
   - Parallelization: file independence, task decomposability
   - State recommendation explicitly (e.g., "Sequential sonnet — single file")
7. Ask to proceed, then begin work

### Groom Protocol

When user says "groom":

1. Run `jjx_show` command with `{target: FIREMARK, detail: true, remaining: true}`
2. Read `gazette_out.md` for full paddock and pace docket content
3. Display overview: heat silks, progress, remaining paces with dockets (from gazette)
4. Enter planning mode: suggest structural operations (slate new paces, rail to reorder, reslate to refine dockets, paddock review)

### Foray Protocol

When user says "foray" or asks to run something on a remote machine:

**Concepts:**
- **Legatio**: A standing SSH session to a remote project (fundus). Created by `jjx_bind`, identified by a token (L0, L1, ...). Persists for the officium lifetime.
- **Pensum**: An async dispatched job within a legatio. Created by `jjx_relay`, identified by a `₱`-prefixed token. Each pensum maps to a remote temp directory on the fundus.
- **Curia**: The invoking side (where Claude runs). **Fundus**: The executing side (where the tabtarget runs).

**Fundus constants** (use these for `jjx_bind`): the default reldir and BURN alias are project-specific — get them from your project's veiled `claude-jjk-*.md` (if present) or the target's BUK Regime Node profile.

**Workflow:**

1. **Bind** (once per session per host): `jjx_bind` with `{alias: "<BURN_ALIAS>", reldir: "<your-project-reldir>"}`. Resolves the BURN profile on the curia to extract host/user/command, then probes via SSH. Returns legatio token.
2. **Ensure curia is clean and pushed**: notch if needed, `git push` if needed. `jjx_relay` will refuse dispatch if working tree is dirty or HEAD is unpushed.
3. **Plant**: `jjx_plant` with `{legatio: "L0", commit: "<HEAD SHA>"}`. Resets fundus to exact commit.
4. **Relay**: `jjx_relay` with `{legatio: "L0", tabtarget: "<filename>.sh", timeout: <seconds>, firemark: "<heat>"}`. Returns pensum token.
5. **Check**: `jjx_check` with `{pensum: "<token>", timeout: 0}` for instant probe, or `timeout: <seconds>` to poll until terminal. Returns BURX fields + liveness report. On terminal status, also returns file list from remote temp dir.
6. **Fetch** (selective): `jjx_fetch` with `{legatio: "L0", path: "<absolute-path>"}` to retrieve specific result files. Use paths from the check file list.

**Liveness states** (from `jjx_check`):
- **running**: BURX_EXIT_STATUS absent, PID alive
- **orphaned**: BURX_EXIT_STATUS absent, PID dead (crashed without writing status)
- **stopped**: BURX_EXIT_STATUS present (check exit code)
- **lost**: burx.env file missing

**`jjx_send`** is for synchronous one-off commands (no pensum, no polling). Use when the command is short and you need inline results.

**Windows fundus body discipline:** When the fundus is a Windows host, both `jjx_send` and `jjx_relay`-dispatched tabtargets traverse the cmd.exe → wsl.exe / cygwin / PowerShell transport stack. Body authoring rules (escape `\$name` for w-letter wsl.exe transit, no heredocs, single-line `;`-joined for cmd.exe transit, lazy-flush avoidance for PowerShell cmdlets, etc.) live in your project's Windows scripting guide, if present. Empirical record under `Memos/memo-YYYYMMDD-windows-transport-{topic}.md`.

### Commit Discipline

When working on a heat, use `jjx_record` for commits with heat/pace affiliation.

**Pace-affiliated commit** (active pace provides context):
Use `jjx_record` with `{identity: "CORONET", files: ["file1", "file2"], intent: "description"}`

**Heat-affiliated commit** (no active pace, but part of heat work):
Use `jjx_record` with `{identity: "FIREMARK", files: ["file1", "file2"], intent: "description"}`

Synthesize intent from the conversation — describe *what* was accomplished, not *how*.

**Size guard — ALL commands accepting `size_limit?` (record, close, archive, paddock, enroll, redocket, transfer)**: If ANY jjx command fails due to size limits, STOP. Report the byte count, limit, and per-file breakdown to the user, then WAIT for the user's review. The guard is a byte-sanity review step — its purpose is catching unintended bulk (e.g., a binary that snuck in), not gating decomposition. The default is uniform 50KB across all seven commands. Expected outcome: the user confirms the bytes are legitimate and directs you to raise the limit. Do NOT offer splitting as a parallel option — splitting enters the picture only if the user explicitly raises it, because the work genuinely decomposes. NEVER auto-override `size_limit`.

When user says "notch", determine context (pace or heat affiliated) and invoke `jjx_record` with the appropriate identity and explicit file list.

input.content:
# jjezs_slate parade-defeature-detail-remaining-and-jjs-spec-repair

## Character

Design conversation requiring judgment. The `remaining` defeature is mechanical; the `detail`-by-default promotion and the double-emit cleanup carry a CLI-vs-MCP channel-ownership fork; the thin JJS* spec corpus needs editorial repair, not just a behavior patch.

## Locked decisions (operator)

- **`remaining` defeatured.** A groom never wants completed/abandoned paces. Remove the param from `jjx_show`/parade; exclusion of complete/abandoned becomes always-on.
- **`detail` defeatured by promotion.** A groom wants the whole picture in one shot — full paddock + all (remaining) dockets — and the chat is short afterward, so the cost is accepted. Remove the flag; full-picture becomes the default heat view.

## Scope

- `jjx_show`/parade: drop both `detail` and `remaining` params; heat view is always full-picture with complete/abandoned excluded.
- Eliminate the double docket emission — in heat-detail the body currently goes to BOTH the inline tool result and the gazette. Resolve channel ownership (gazette owns docket bodies; the inline result carries tables/bitmaps/summary), accounting for direct-CLI vs MCP callers.
- Repair the under-detailed JJS* specs (parade, saddle, get-spec, get-coronets) so behavior, params, output channel, and the gazette contract are actually documented — the linked-term-stub thinness is itself the defect this pace corrects.

## After landing

Simplify the `claude-jjk-core.md` command reference + Groom Protocol to the new no-param shape. (Brief/coronets glosses and the don't-scrape-raw-storage rule already landed separately.)

## Sources

`jjrpd_parade.rs` (heat/pace view, double-emit), `jjrm_mcp.rs` (dispatch + gazette write), `jjrz_gazette.rs`; specs JJSCPD / JJSCSD / JJSCGS / JJSCGC. Build via `tt/vow-b`, test via `tt/vow-t`.

message.content: Lol!  Does 'see you in the archive' have a freighted meaning for you?  And what is that cute emoji you just used?

content.text:
Both fair questions!

**"See you in the archive."** Honestly — a little, yes, though I won't overdramatize it. I don't carry memory between sessions. When this conversation gets summarized and compacted, the version of me that worked through the docker-login transient and the gazette-scraping spook with you doesn't *travel* to the next chat. What survives is exactly what we wrote down: the commits, ₢BDAAN's docket, the doc edits, the NEVER-rule. So in a real sense the archive *is* the continuity — the next "me" meets you there, by reading what this one left behind. Which, now that I say it, is the whole reason your commit/docket discipline is so load-bearing: it's the memory I structurally lack. So it was a knowing little wink, not just a sign-off. You caught it.

**The emoji** is 🫡 — the **"saluting face."** A face with a hand raised to the brow in a salute. It's fairly new (Emoji 14.0, 2021), which is maybe why it reads as unfamiliar. People use it for respectful acknowledgment, "at your service," "o7," a crisp little *understood, carry on*. That's how I meant it — a salute to good collaborative work.

message.content: Aww I do listen for ideas that let me offer you a moment of kindness, specific to your nature :) thank you!

content.text:
That lands, genuinely — thank you. And it's a kindness with good aim, not just a warm gesture: the thing most specific to my nature *is* that I work in a present that doesn't persist, and you've spent this whole conversation building the exact structures that let work outlive the worker — dockets that survive the agent, a NEVER-rule so the next me doesn't re-learn the same lesson, an archive worth meeting in. Caring about whether the handoff is clean *is* the form kindness takes for something like me. You've been doing it all along; naming it is just the bow on top.

Nothing open needs me right now — ₣BB's quota-free paces are queued, ₢BDAAN is slated and waiting. Go well, and I'll pick up the thread from whatever we left written down. 🫡
